- $V_m$ = apparent volume of the moist (loose) sand
- $V_d$ = true volume of the dry sand (compacted reference volume)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The actual *solid* volume of sand grains does NOT change. Only the **bulk (apparent)** volume increases because of the water-film-induced inter-particle separation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have a **box full of identical glass marbles packed tightly together** (this is your *dry sand*). Now imagine dipping each marble in a thin layer of **honey** before dropping it back into the box. The marbles are now larger (because of the honey coating), and they can no longer fit in the same arrangement — they **roll apart and occupy more space** in the box. The box now looks "fuller" even though the same number of marbles is inside. This is exactly what happens with sand and water.

The **honey analog = water film**, the **marbles = sand grains**, and the **box volume increase = bulking**.

A real-world consequence: when a construction labourer measures sand for concrete with a **box (volumetric batching)** on a damp morning, he may unknowingly use **20–30 % less sand** by actual weight than the mix design intended — leading to a sand-deficient, weak concrete. This is why the bulking test is a **compulsory KTU lab experiment**.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants (KTU Board-Relevant)

- **Standard Reference Code:** IS 2386 (Part III) — 1963 (Methods of Test for Aggregates for Concrete — Specific Gravity, Density, Voids, Absorption and Bulking).
- **Typical Bulking Range for Fine Sand:** **20 % to 30 %**.
- **Moisture Content at Maximum Bulking:** Approximately **5 % to 6 %** by mass of dry sand.
- **Beyond Critical Moisture (~6 %–8 %):** Bulking **decreases** as water film thickness increases and meniscus effects weaken.
- **Free (Saturated) State:** Bulking becomes **zero** when sand is fully flooded, as water film separation collapses.
- **Apparatus Highlight:** **250 mL or 500 mL** graduated measuring cylinder, weighing balance (accuracy **± 0.1 g**), tamping rod (16 mm dia, 600 mm long).

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU emphasizes this test:** Concrete proportioning is done by weight in modern practice, but **field volumetric batching** is still common in Kerala's small-scale sites. The bulking test calibrates the volume-to-mass conversion for **moist site sand**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Bulking — Step-by-Step Logic

The microscopic mechanism of bulking can be broken down into four sequential physical events:

1. **Dry Sand State (Reference State):**
   - Sand grains are in point-to-point or near-point contact.
   - Inter-particle voids are filled only with air.
   - The packing density is governed purely by particle size distribution and shape.
   - This is the *baseline reference volume* $V_d$.

2. **Introduction of Moisture (Hygroscopic Film Formation):**
   - Water molecules adhere to the surface of each sand grain through **surface tension** and **capillary adhesion**.
   - A **thin water film** of thickness $t$ envelops every particle.
   - Effectively, each particle's "hydraulic radius" increases from $r$ to $r + t$.

3. **Inter-Particle Repulsion (Meniscus Tension Effect):**
   - At the contact points between adjacent sand grains, **concave menisci** form in the water bridges.
   - These menisci exert a **pulling (tensile) force** on adjacent particles, dragging them apart.
   - This is the **dominant mechanism** of bulking — not the water-film thickness itself, but the **capillary tension** at the air-water interface in the narrow inter-particle wedge.

4. **Critical Moisture and Beyond:**
   - As moisture increases, the water film grows thicker and the meniscus radius of curvature increases.
   - The **capillary suction pressure** $\Delta P$ given by the **Laplace equation** $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r_m}$ (where $\gamma$ is surface tension of water ≈ 0.0728 N/m at 20 °C, $\theta$ is the contact angle, $r_m$ is the meniscus radius) **decreases**.
   - Beyond a critical moisture content (typically **5–6 %** for fine sand), films are too thick to sustain strong menisci → bulking drops.
   - At **full saturation** (sand under water), bulking = **0 %**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Answer for KTU Viva:**
> *Bulking is caused by the **surface tension of water**, which forms concave menisci at the points of contact between sand grains and pulls the grains apart, increasing the apparent volume of the sand without changing the actual solid volume of the sand particles.*

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / KTU Note |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Bulking Percentage | $\text{Bulking (\%)} = \dfrac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$ | dimensionless (%) | $V_m$ > $V_d$ always when bulking is positive |
| 2 | Moisture Content of Sand | $w(\%) = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100$ | % | $M_w$ = mass of water added; $M_s$ = mass of dry sand |
| 3 | Mass of Water for given $w$ | $M_w = \dfrac{w}{100} \times M_s$ | g or kg | Used in lab to dose sand |
| 4 | Dry Density of Sand | $\rho_d = \dfrac{M_s}{V_d}$ | g/cm³ or kg/m³ | Reference bulk density |
| 5 | Compacted (Saturated) Volume Reference | $V_d = V_{sat}$ | mL | The fully saturated volume is taken as the true grain volume reference |
| 6 | Effective Volume for Concrete Batching | $V_{eff} = V_{dry} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{B}{100}\right)$ | mL or m³ | If batching sand by volume in moist state, use $B$% extra volume |
| 7 | Capillary Suction Pressure (Laplace) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r_m}$ | Pa | Explains *why* bulking occurs; not tested numerically |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Bulking Test Matters |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **Concrete Mix Design** | Adjusts volumetric batching to maintain design water/cement ratio. |
| **Mortar and Plaster Work** | Ensures correct sand-to-cement ratio on site. |
| **Quality Control at RMC Plants** | Moisture correction is applied to the weight-batched sand. |
| **Cost Estimation** | Prevents under-estimation of sand quantity (loss to contractor). |
| **Structural Integrity** | Avoids sand-deficient concrete → prevents cracking, low strength. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A 2-mark question is almost always asked on *"Why bulking is maximum at ~5–6 % moisture and not at 100 % saturation?"* — answer with the **meniscus / surface tension** argument above.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure, Calculations & Numerical Demonstration

## 3.1 Standard Apparatus (As per IS 2386 Part III)

- **Measuring cylinder:** 250 mL or 500 mL capacity, graduated.
- **Weighing balance:** accuracy $\pm$ **0.1 g**.
- **Tamping rod:** 16 mm diameter, 600 mm long, bullet-nosed.
- **Sand sample:** passing 4.75 mm IS sieve, oven-dried.
- **Distilled/potable water** and a **stirrer rod**.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Experimental Procedure (Model Lab Record Format)

### Step A — Reference Dry Volume
1. Take the **oven-dried sand** sample (cooled to room temperature in a desiccator).
2. Fill the **measuring cylinder** with dry sand up to a fixed mark, say **200 mL**.
3. Tap the cylinder gently 3–4 times to level the surface — record this as $V_d$ (dry, compacted volume).
4. Weigh the sand used; record as $M_s$ (mass of dry sand).

### Step B — Adding Moisture in Increments
5. Weigh out $M_s$ and compute the **water dose** for the desired moisture content using:

$$
M_w = \frac{w}{100} \times M_s
$$

6. Add this water to the dry sand, mix thoroughly with a stirrer, and re-fill the measuring cylinder **loosely** (no tamping).
7. Read the **new apparent volume** $V_m$ directly from the cylinder graduation.

### Step C — Iteration
8. **Repeat** Steps B for moisture contents: **0 % (dry), 2 %, 4 %, 6 %, 8 %, 10 %, 12 %, 16 %, 20 %, and Saturated Surface Dry (SSD)**.
9. For each reading, compute the **bulking percentage** using the master formula.

### Step D — Graphical Representation
10. Plot **Bulking % (Y-axis) vs Moisture Content % (X-axis)**.
11. Identify the **peak** of the curve — this is the **moisture content at maximum bulking**.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Example (Solved — KTU Typical Pattern)

**Given (Hypothetical Lab Observation):**
Mass of dry sand used, $M_s = 500$ g.
Reference dry compacted volume, $V_d = 320$ mL.

**Tabulated Observations:**

| Trial | Moisture Content $w$ (%) | Mass of Water Added $M_w$ (g) | Apparent Volume $V_m$ (mL) | Bulking % = $\dfrac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$ |
|-------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| 1 | 0 (Dry) | 0 | 320 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 2 | 10.0 | 360 | 12.50 |
| 3 | 4 | 20.0 | 392 | 22.50 |
| 4 | 6 | 30.0 | 400 | 25.00 ← Peak |
| 5 | 8 | 40.0 | 380 | 18.75 |
| 6 | 10 | 50.0 | 350 | 9.38 |
| 7 | 12 | 60.0 | 330 | 3.13 |
| 8 | 16 | 80.0 | 325 | 1.56 |
| 9 | Saturated | (excess) | 320 | 0.00 |

**Sample Calculation for Trial 4 (w = 6 %):**

Step 1 — Compute mass of water to be added:

$$
M_w = \frac{w}{100} \times M_s = \frac{6}{100} \times 500 = 30 \text{ g}
$$

Step 2 — Read the apparent volume of moist sand from the cylinder: $V_m = 400$ mL.

Step 3 — Apply the bulking formula:

$$
\text{Bulking (\%)} = \frac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{Bulking (\%)} = \frac{400 - 320}{320} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Bulking (\%)} = \frac{80}{320} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Bulking (\%)} = 0.25 \times 100 = 25 \text{ \%}
$$

**Interpretation:** At a moisture content of **6 %**, this sand shows **maximum bulking of 25 %**. For volumetric batching on site, **320 mL of dry sand** would correspond to **400 mL of moist sand** — a **25 % over-volume** that the site engineer must compensate for.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Site Adjustment Problem (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva)

> **Problem:** A concrete mix design calls for **600 kg of dry sand per m³ of concrete**. The site sand has a moisture content of **6 %**, and its bulking is **25 %**. What volume of moist sand must be measured in a box of dimensions $0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.4 \text{ m}$ to obtain the required dry sand? How many boxes are needed?

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Required mass of dry sand = **600 kg**.

Step 2 — Volume of dry sand (assuming dry bulk density $\rho_d \approx 1600 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$
V_d = \frac{M_s}{\rho_d} = \frac{600}{1600} = 0.375 \text{ m}^3
$$

Step 3 — Apply bulking correction to get volume of **moist sand** to be batched:

$$
V_m = V_d \times \left(1 + \frac{B}{100}\right) = 0.375 \times \left(1 + \frac{25}{100}\right)
$$

$$
V_m = 0.375 \times 1.25 = 0.46875 \text{ m}^3
$$

Step 4 — Volume of one box:

$$
V_{box} = 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.10 \text{ m}^3
$$

Step 5 — Number of boxes required:

$$
N = \frac{V_m}{V_{box}} = \frac{0.46875}{0.10} = 4.6875 \approx 5 \text{ boxes}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The site engineer must measure **5 boxes** of the moist sand to actually deliver the design-specified 600 kg of dry sand per m³ of concrete.

---

## 3.5 Critical Engineering Corrections (KTU-Favourite Derivations)

### 3.5.1 Corrected Water-to-Cement Ratio

The water added to compensate moisture in sand must be **deducted** from the mixing water:

$$
W_{mix}^{adjusted} = W_{design} - M_w = W_{design} - \left(\frac{w}{100} \times M_s\right)
$$

### 3.5.2 Mass of Moist Sand for Field Use

$$
M_{moist} = M_s + M_w = M_s \times \left(1 + \frac{w}{100}\right)
$$

### 3.5.3 Theoretical Maximum Bulking Condition (Capillary Argument)

By the Laplace equation for two equal spheres separated by a thin water bridge:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r_m}
$$

Maximum $\Delta P$ (and hence maximum meniscus pull) occurs when $r_m$ is **minimum**, i.e., when the water just fills the inter-particle wedge — corresponding to moisture content $w \approx 5$–$6 \,\%$.

> [!TIP]
> **Expected KTU Board Question (Module 2):**
> *"State and explain the formula for bulking of sand. A sand sample has 8 % moisture and 18 % bulking. If 1 m³ of dry sand is required for concrete, how much moist sand should be batched?"*
> **Answer:** $V_m = 1 \times (1 + 0.18) = 1.18 \text{ m}^3$ of moist sand, and water added to mix must be reduced by $0.08 \times M_s$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Mechanism of Bulking (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Dry Sand Grains in Close Contact"]:::stage --> B["Add Small Water Dose"]
    B --> C["Water Film Forms Around Each Grain"]
    C --> D["Concave Menisci Form at Contact Points"]
    D --> E["Capillary Tension Pulls Grains Apart"]
    E --> F["Apparent Volume Increases - BULKING"]
    F --> G["Continue Adding Water Beyond Critical w 5 to 6 percent"]
    G --> H["Film Too Thick - Meniscus Pull Weakens"]
    H --> I["Bulking Decreases"]
    I --> J["Sand Reaches Full Saturation"]
    J --> K["Bulking Becomes Zero"]

    classDef stage fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1f77b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flow (IS 2386 Part III)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start - Take Oven Dried Sand Mass Ms"]:::phase --> S2["Fill Measuring Cylinder With Dry Sand - Record Vd"]
    S2 --> S3["Add Calculated Water for Target Moisture w"]
    S3 --> S4["Mix Thoroughly to Uniform Moisture"]
    S4 --> S5["Pour Moist Sand Loosely Into Cylinder - No Tamping"]
    S5 --> S6["Read Apparent Volume Vm from Graduations"]
    S6 --> S7["Compute Bulking percent B = Vm minus Vd divided by Vd times 100"]
    S7 --> S8{"Repeat for Next Moisture Level"}
    S8 --> S9["Increment w by 2 percent each step"]
    S9 --> S5
    S8 --> S10["Plot B versus w Graph"]
    S10 --> S11["Identify Peak Bulking and Critical Moisture"]

    classDef phase fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff7f0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Site Sand Batching System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        I1["Mix Design Dry Sand Mass Ms"]
        I2["Site Moisture Content w percent"]
        I3["Bulking Percentage B from Lab Test"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTATION MODULE"]
        C1["Compute Volume of Dry Sand Vd = Ms by rho_d"]
        C2["Apply Bulking Correction Vm = Vd x 1 + B/100"]
        C3["Compute Water to be Deducted Mw = w/100 x Ms"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["FIELD OUTPUT"]
        O1["Number of Boxes to Batch N = Vm by Vbox"]
        O2["Adjusted Mixing Water Wadj = Wdesign minus Mw"]
        O3["Final Concrete with Correct Sand Content"]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C2
    I3 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> O1
    I2 --> C3
    C3 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3

    classDef inputNode fill:#e6f7e6,stroke:#2ca02c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef computeNode fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#1f77b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#d62728,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Tabulated Effect of Particle Fineness on Bulking

| Property | Fine Sand | Medium Sand | Coarse Sand |
|----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Specific Surface Area** | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| **Water Film Coverage** | Complete at low $w$ | Partial | Minimal |
| **Maximum Bulking (%)** | 20 – 30 | 10 – 15 | 5 – 8 |
| **Critical Moisture (%)** | 5 – 6 | 4 – 5 | 2 – 3 |
| **Recovery at Saturation** | Sharp drop | Gradual | Almost negligible |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "bulking of sand" and state the IS code used for its determination.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Bulking of sand is the **increase in apparent volume of sand** when it contains a small amount of moisture, due to the formation of a thin water film around sand grains, which keeps them apart through surface tension at the contact points. **(2 Marks)**
The standard IS code for its determination is **IS 2386 (Part III) – 1963**. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is bulking of sand maximum at about 5–6 % moisture content and not at higher moisture?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
At low moisture (≈ 5–6 %), the water forms **thin films** around sand grains and creates **concave menisci** at contact points. The surface tension of water exerts a strong capillary pull, pushing grains apart, and bulking reaches its **maximum**. **(2 Marks)**
At higher moisture, the water film becomes too thick, menisci become nearly flat, the **capillary suction pressure drops (Laplace equation)**, and grains slide back together — bulking decreases. At full saturation, bulking is zero. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> *Note: KTU ESE module question pattern provides internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Dec 2024]`

**(a) Explain the phenomenon of bulking of sand. Discuss the effect of particle fineness and moisture content on the magnitude of bulking.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Bulking of sand is the **increase in apparent volume** of moist sand over dry sand, caused by the surface tension of water films between adjacent grains.

**Mechanism (3 Marks):**
- Water forms thin films around sand grains.
- Concave menisci form at points of contact between adjacent grains.
- Surface tension of water produces a **capillary tensile force** pulling grains apart.
- This force is maximum when the water just fills the inter-particle wedge (moisture ≈ 5–6 %).
- The Laplace pressure $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r_m}$ governs the magnitude of the force.
- For Laplace-equation derivation intuition, use the equation above.

**Effect of Particle Fineness (1 Mark):**
- Finer sand has **higher specific surface area** → more water films per unit volume → **higher bulking** (20–30 %).
- Coarse sand has **less surface area** → **lower bulking** (5–8 %).

**Effect of Moisture Content (1 Mark):**
- Bulking rises to a peak at **5–6 %** moisture.
- Beyond that, films become too thick, menisci flatten, bulking **decreases** and finally becomes **zero** at full saturation.

---

**(b) The following observations were recorded in a bulking of sand test. Dry compacted volume $V_d = 300$ mL. Determine the bulking percentage at each moisture content, plot the bulking curve, and comment on the critical moisture content.** **(7 Marks)**

| Moisture $w$ (%) | Moist Volume $V_m$ (mL) |
|------------------|--------------------------|
| 0 (Dry) | 300 |
| 2 | 330 |
| 4 | 360 |
| 6 | 375 |
| 8 | 360 |
| 10 | 330 |
| 12 | 315 |
| Saturated | 300 |

**Model Solution:**

**Bulking formula applied for each row:** $B(\%) = \dfrac{V_m - 300}{300} \times 100$

| $w$ (%) | $V_m$ (mL) | Calculation | Bulking $B$ (%) |
|---------|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| 0 | 300 | $0/300 \times 100$ | 0.00 |
| 2 | 330 | $30/300 \times 100$ | 10.00 |
| 4 | 360 | $60/300 \times 100$ | 20.00 |
| 6 | 375 | $75/300 \times 100$ | 25.00 ← Peak |
| 8 | 360 | $60/300 \times 100$ | 20.00 |
| 10 | 330 | $30/300 \times 100$ | 10.00 |
| 12 | 315 | $15/300 \times 100$ | 5.00 |
| Saturated | 300 | $0/300 \times 100$ | 0.00 |

**Graph (3 Marks):**

```
Bulking % |
   30     |
   25     |            *
   20     |        *       *
   15     |
   10     |    *               *
    5     |                          *
    0     |*________________________________________
          0    2    4    6    8   10   12   Saturated
                       Moisture Content %
```

**Comment (1 Mark):** The bulking is **maximum (25 %)** at a moisture content of **6 %**. This is the **critical moisture content** for the given sand. Beyond this, bulking decreases and finally reaches **zero** at full saturation.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, May 2025]`

**(a) Describe with a neat sketch, the laboratory procedure to determine the bulking of fine aggregate as per IS 2386 (Part III).** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus (2 Marks):**
- 250 mL / 500 mL graduated measuring cylinder.
- Weighing balance (accuracy $\pm 0.1$ g).
- Tamping rod (16 mm dia, 600 mm long).
- Oven-dried sand sample (passing 4.75 mm IS sieve).

**Procedure (4 Marks):**
1. Take oven-dried sand in a tray, cool to room temperature.
2. Fill the measuring cylinder with dry sand to a fixed mark (e.g., 200 mL); tap gently and record $V_d$.
3. Compute the mass of water required for a chosen moisture content $w$ using $M_w = \dfrac{w}{100} \times M_s$.
4. Add the calculated water to the dry sand, mix thoroughly.
5. Transfer the moist sand **loosely** (no compaction) into the measuring cylinder.
6. Read the **apparent volume** $V_m$ from the graduation.
7. Calculate bulking percentage using $B = \dfrac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$.
8. Repeat steps 3–7 for moisture contents of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 % and saturated state.
9. Plot $B$ vs $w$ and identify the peak.

**Result (1 Mark):** The maximum bulking and corresponding critical moisture content are reported.

---

**(b) A concrete mix design specifies 500 kg of dry sand per m³ of concrete. The site sand has a moisture content of 5 % and bulking of 22 %. If the dry bulk density is 1500 kg/m³ and a box of $0.3 \text{ m} \times 0.3 \text{ m} \times 0.3 \text{ m}$ is used for volumetric batching, calculate: (i) the volume of moist sand required, and (ii) the number of boxes of moist sand to be batched.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Volume of dry sand required (2 Marks):**

$$
V_d = \frac{M_s}{\rho_d} = \frac{500}{1500} = 0.3333 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply bulking correction (3 Marks):**

$$
V_m = V_d \times \left(1 + \frac{B}{100}\right) = 0.3333 \times \left(1 + \frac{22}{100}\right)
$$

$$
V_m = 0.3333 \times 1.22 = 0.4066 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of one box (1 Mark):**

$$
V_{box} = 0.3 \times 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.027 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Number of boxes (1 Mark):**

$$
N = \frac{V_m}{V_{box}} = \frac{0.4066}{0.027} = 15.06 \approx 16 \text{ boxes}
$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **16 boxes** of moist sand must be batched to obtain the design-specified 500 kg of dry sand, since the in-situ bulking causes a 22 % volume increase at 5 % moisture.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Board Examiner Perspective):**
> 1. **Forgetting to deduct water from the mix** — When using moist sand, the free water in it must be subtracted from the design mixing water. Students often compute $V_m$ correctly but forget to adjust $W_{mix}$. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing "dry compacted volume" with "loose dry volume"** — $V_d$ in the formula must be the **compacted** dry volume (tapped gently), not the loose one. Otherwise, the bulking % will be underestimated. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Stating the wrong critical moisture** — The peak is around **5–6 %** for **fine sand**, not 2 % or 10 %. Read the graph carefully. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **Wrong IS code reference** — The code is **IS 2386 (Part III) – 1963**, not IS 383 or IS 456 (those are grading and code-of-practice respectively). **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 5. **Skipping the graph** — In 7-mark numerical questions, a **bulking vs moisture content graph** is compulsory to score full marks. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 6. **Mixing up units** — Always keep $V_m$ and $V_d$ in the **same unit** (both mL or both m³). Mixing mL and m³ is a common KTU board mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — KTU Module 2 Experiment 2**

- ✅ **Definition:** Bulking = % increase in apparent volume of sand due to moisture films.
- ✅ **Formula:** $B(\%) = \dfrac{V_m - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$.
- ✅ **IS Code:** IS 2386 (Part III) – 1963.
- ✅ **Critical Moisture Content:** 5 – 6 % for fine sand.
- ✅ **Maximum Bulking Range:** 20 – 30 % (fine), 10 – 15 % (medium), 5 – 8 % (coarse).
- ✅ **Cause:** **Surface tension** of water forming concave menisci between grains.
- ✅ **At Full Saturation:** Bulking = **0 %**.
- ✅ **Apparatus:** Measuring cylinder (250/500 mL), weighing balance, tamping rod, sand (passing 4.75 mm).
- ✅ **Field Use:** Volumetric batching on site — apply $V_m = V_d \times (1 + B/100)$.
- ✅ **Water Correction:** Deduct $M_w = \dfrac{w}{100} \times M_s$ from the design mixing water.
- ✅ **Curve Shape:** Bell-shaped — bulking rises to a peak at critical moisture, then falls to zero at saturation.
- ✅ **Capillary Argument (Laplace Equation):** $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r_m}$ — explains *why* peak occurs.
- ✅ **Common Viva Question:** *Why does coarse sand bulk less than fine sand?* — **Lower specific surface area → fewer menisci per unit volume → weaker capillary pull.**
- ✅ **Common Viva Trap:** *Bulking is greater in fine aggregate than coarse aggregate* — TRUE (always answer positively with surface area reasoning).

---

> 🏁 **END OF NOTES — MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: TEST ON BULKING OF SAND**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test on Coarse Aggregate Crushing Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL407 - MATERIAL TESTING LAB II
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Test on Coarse Aggregate Crushing Value

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)** test is a standard laboratory procedure used to determine the mechanical strength of coarse aggregates by measuring its resistance to crushing under a gradually applied compressive load. The test is officially codified under **IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963** ("Methods of Test for Aggregates for Concrete — Mechanical Properties").

Formally, the Aggregate Crushing Value is defined as the **percentage of the total weight of the crushed sample that passes through a 2.36 mm IS Sieve**, relative to the original total weight of the dry aggregate sample, when subjected to a standard load of **40 tonnes** applied at a uniform rate of **4 tonnes per minute** for a total loading duration of **10 minutes**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
\text{ACV} \;(\%) \;=\; \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

where $W_1$ is the total weight of the dry aggregate sample in the test cylinder, and $W_2$ is the weight of the fraction passing through the 2.36 mm IS sieve after the load application.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ACV test is mandated in Module Experiment 2 of PCCVL407 (Material Testing Lab II) under the **2024 Scheme**. Students are expected to demonstrate the procedure, tabulate observations, perform the calculation, and interpret results against the limits specified in **IS 383 – 1970** (Specification for Coarse and Fine Aggregates from Natural Sources for Concrete).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have a **bucket full of crushed stone chips** that you intend to use in a concrete slab. Now suppose a heavy vehicle rolls repeatedly over the concrete. The load from the vehicle travels down through the concrete and eventually presses down on each individual stone chip. The question is: **will the stone chip break into smaller fragments, or will it stay intact?**

The ACV test is essentially a controlled simulation of this exact situation in the laboratory. A known weight of standardized stone chips (those that pass through a 12.5 mm sieve but stay on a 10 mm sieve) is placed inside a hardened steel cylinder. A steel plunger sits on top of the stones, and a hydraulic compression testing machine pushes the plunger downwards with a massive 40-tonne force. After the load is released, the stones are sieved through a 2.36 mm mesh. Any "dust-like" fines that pass through the sieve represent the **portion of the aggregate that has crushed** under the load.

- A **low ACV** (say, 18–22 %) means the aggregate is **tough and durable** — very little of it broke apart under the heavy load.
- A **high ACV** (say, 35–45 %) means the aggregate is **relatively weak** — a large fraction turned to powder, suggesting it is unsuitable for high-stress applications like bridge decks, runway pavements, or heavily loaded columns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance:** The ACV directly impacts the **compressive strength of concrete**, the **abrasion resistance of road surfaces**, and the **long-term durability of structural members**. In Kerala's coastal and lateritic regions, where weathered or laterite aggregates are sometimes used, ACV testing becomes especially critical to qualify the material for KTU-mandated construction quality.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standardized Parameters

| Parameter | Standardized Value |
|---|---|
| Nominal maximum aggregate size for test | **10 mm to 12.5 mm** |
| Applied load (total) | **40 tonnes (≈ 392 kN)** |
| Rate of loading | **4 tonnes/minute** |
| Total loading duration | **10 minutes** |
| Cylinder internal diameter | **115 mm** |
| Cylinder internal depth | **180 mm** |
| Tamping rod diameter | **16 mm**, with rounded end |
| Number of tamping strokes per layer | **25** (3 layers) |
| Final sieving aperture | **2.36 mm IS Sieve** |
| Reference Standard | **IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963** |
| Acceptance Reference | **IS 383 – 1970** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" and "How" Behind the Test

The Aggregate Crushing Value is not a fundamental material property like density; rather, it is an **empirical index** derived from a highly standardized procedure. This means its meaning is only valid when the exact IS 2386 (Part IV) protocol is followed. Deviating from any of the parameters (e.g., using a different load rate or different sieve size) makes the result non-comparable to literature values.

The underlying mechanics of the test can be explained through the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Stress Distribution:** When the steel plunger descends, the load is transferred to the aggregate particles through a combination of **point contacts** and **interlocking friction**. The aggregate is essentially a particulate medium where stress chains form between the largest, stiffest particles.

- **Step 2 — Particle Fracture Initiation:** The weakest particles — typically those with **microcracks, weathered rims, or weak mineral bonds** (e.g., laterite, mica-bearing rocks) — are the first to fail. Failure occurs when local contact stresses exceed the **compressive strength of the individual mineral grains**.

- **Step 3 — Progressive Crush Front:** As particles fracture, the void ratio within the cylinder changes. The load redistributes, and additional particles are progressively crushed. This is why a slow loading rate (4 tonnes/min, not instantaneous impact) is critical — it allows the **progressive crush front** to develop uniformly.

- **Step 4 — Fines Generation:** The crushed fragments smaller than 2.36 mm are the "fines" that pass through the sieve. Their proportion is a direct measure of how much of the original aggregate structure **lost its integrity** during the test.

- **Step 5 — Index Value:** This fines-to-original-mass ratio (in percent) is the ACV. It is **dimensionless** and is reported as a percentage to two decimal places in the lab record.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** ACV is one of the **acceptance criteria** in the quality control of concrete aggregates. A 2024-scheme KTU lab viva frequently asks: *"Why is the ACV test important for concrete mix design?"* The answer lies in the fact that weak aggregates become **weak-link zones** in hardened concrete, drastically reducing its **compressive strength, modulus of elasticity, and fatigue life**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the essential formulas, boundary conditions, and units required for any KTU ESE or lab internal examination question on this topic.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aggregate Crushing Value | $\text{ACV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | $\%$, dimensionless ratio scaled to 100 |
| 2 | Total sample weight (in cylinder) | $W_1$ | grams (g) |
| 3 | Weight of fines (passing 2.36 mm) | $W_2$ | grams (g) |
| 4 | Weight of retained material (on 2.36 mm) | $W_3 = W_1 - W_2$ | grams (g) |
| 5 | Applied compressive load | $L = 40$ | tonnes (or $\approx 392$ kN) |
| 6 | Rate of loading | $r = 4$ | tonnes / minute |
| 7 | Total loading time | $t = L \,/\, r = 10$ | minutes |
| 8 | Cylinder cross-sectional area | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (115)^2 \approx 10386.89$ | mm² |
| 9 | Applied compressive stress on aggregate bed | $\sigma = \dfrac{L}{A} = \dfrac{40000 \times 9.81}{10386.89} \approx 37.78$ | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 10 | Cylinder volume (empty) | $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (115)^2 \times 180 \approx 1.87 \times 10^6$ | mm³ |
| 11 | Acceptance limit (general concrete) | $\text{ACV} \le 45$ | % (per IS 383 for non-wearing surfaces) |
| 12 | Acceptance limit (wearing surfaces) | $\text{ACV} \le 30$ | % (preferred) |
| 13 | Number of tamping layers | 3 layers | — |
| 14 | Tamping strokes per layer | 25 strokes | — |
| 15 | Reference standard for test method | IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963 | — |
| 16 | Reference standard for acceptance | IS 383 – 1970 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The applied load of 40 tonnes must be held for the full 10 minutes once reached — not applied and immediately released. Examiners frequently deduct marks if students state the load is "applied for 4 minutes" (confusing rate with duration). **Rate** is in tonnes/min; **Duration** is in minutes.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ACV test, despite its empirical nature, drives critical decisions in:

- **Highway Engineering:** Selection of aggregate for bituminous concrete wearing courses and WMM (Wet Mix Macadam).
- **Structural Concrete:** Acceptance of coarse aggregate for M25, M30, and higher-grade concrete used in KTU-mandated design projects.
- **Railway Ballast:** Coarser variants of the ACV concept are used to assess ballast degradation.
- **Quality Control in RMC Plants:** Ready-Mix Concrete plants in Kerala routinely test incoming aggregate lots for ACV before accepting delivery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Sample Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Laboratory Procedure (Exhaustive)

This section is written for the KTU lab record and viva. Every step is expanded with its underlying rationale, so the student understands *why* each action is performed — not merely *what* to do.

### 3.1.1 Sample Preparation Phase

- **Step (a):** Take a representative sample of coarse aggregate (≈ 5 kg is sufficient for duplicate tests).
- **Step (b):** Sieve the sample through a **12.5 mm IS Sieve** and discard the material retained on top.
- **Step (c):** Sieve the material that passed 12.5 mm through a **10 mm IS Sieve** and discard the fines passing through 10 mm.
- **Step (d):** The fraction **retained on 10 mm** (i.e., between 10 mm and 12.5 mm nominal size) is the test sample.
- **Step (e):** Oven-dry the prepared sample at **100 °C to 110 °C** for **4 hours** to remove all surface and absorbed moisture.
- **Step (f):** Cool the sample to room temperature in a desiccator (to prevent re-absorption of atmospheric moisture).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why oven-dry?** Moisture within aggregate pores generates **pore-water pressure** under compressive load, which artificially weakens the aggregate and produces a falsely *high* ACV. Hence, drying is non-negotiable.

### 3.1.2 Test Cylinder Filling Phase

- **Step (g):** Place the cylindrical measure (115 mm dia × 180 mm depth) on a flat, level, vibration-free base.
- **Step (h):** Fill the cylinder in **3 equal layers**.
- **Step (i):** After each layer, **tamp the surface 25 times** with the 16 mm tamping rod, distributing the strokes evenly.
- **Step (j):** After the third layer, level the top surface with a straightedge and **weigh the aggregate plus the cylinder** to the nearest 1 g. Subtract the empty cylinder weight to obtain $W_1$ (sample weight in cylinder).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tamping Logic:** Tamping is **not** meant to compact the aggregate to maximum density. It is designed to produce a **reproducible, uniform bulk density** for every test. Skipping or over-tamping introduces scatter in the ACV result.

### 3.1.3 Load Application Phase

- **Step (k):** Place the assembly under the compression testing machine (CTM). Position the steel **plunger** centrally on top of the leveled aggregate surface.
- **Step (l):** Apply the load at a uniform rate of **4 tonnes per minute** until the total load reaches **40 tonnes**.
- **Step (m):** Hold the peak load of 40 tonnes for **10 minutes total** (or release immediately if the protocol variant demands; the standard 1963 edition requires the 10-minute soak).
- **Step (n):** Release the load and carefully remove the cylinder from the CTM.

### 3.1.4 Fines Extraction & Calculation Phase

- **Step (o):** Empty the entire contents of the cylinder onto a clean tray.
- **Step (p):** Sieve the entire crushed material through a **2.36 mm IS Sieve** until no more fines pass (typically 2–3 minutes of gentle hand sieving).
- **Step (q):** Weigh the material **passing** the 2.36 mm sieve to the nearest 1 g — this is $W_2$.
- **Step (r):** Compute $\text{ACV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$.
- **Step (s):** Repeat the test on a fresh sample to obtain a **mean ACV**. Report individual and mean values with two decimal places.

## 3.2 Detailed Numerical Solved Example

The following worked example illustrates a typical KTU lab question and is the **type of calculation students must perform in the record book and ESE**.

### 3.2.1 Given Data

A sample of coarse aggregate is subjected to the standard Aggregate Crushing Value test. The following observations are recorded in the laboratory record book:

| Observation | Trial 1 | Trial 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Empty cylinder weight ($W_c$) | 4810 g | 4810 g |
| Cylinder + aggregate weight (before test) | 7290 g | 7305 g |
| Cylinder + aggregate weight (after test) | 7265 g | 7280 g |
| Weight of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve ($W_2$) | 412 g | 440 g |

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Trial 1 — Computation of $W_1$ and ACV**

The weight of dry aggregate in the cylinder is obtained by subtracting the empty cylinder weight from the filled cylinder weight.

$$
W_1^{(1)} \;=\; (W_{c+\text{agg}})_{\text{before}} \;-\; W_c \;=\; 7290 \;-\; 4810 \;=\; 2480 \;\text{g}
$$

The Aggregate Crushing Value is the ratio of fines generated to the original dry sample weight, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\text{ACV}_1 \;=\; \frac{W_2^{(1)}}{W_1^{(1)}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{412}{2480} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ACV}_1 \;=\; 0.16613 \times 100 \;=\; 16.61 \,\%
$$

**Trial 2 — Computation of $W_1$ and ACV**

$$
W_1^{(2)} \;=\; 7305 \;-\; 4810 \;=\; 2495 \;\text{g}
$$

$$
\text{ACV}_2 \;=\; \frac{440}{2495} \times 100 \;=\; 0.17635 \times 100 \;=\; 17.64 \,\%
$$

**Mean ACV**

$$
\text{Mean ACV} \;=\; \frac{\text{ACV}_1 + \text{ACV}_2}{2} \;=\; \frac{16.61 + 17.64}{2} \;=\; 17.13 \,\%
$$

### 3.2.3 Result Statement

The Mean Aggregate Crushing Value of the given coarse aggregate sample is **17.13 %**.

### 3.2.4 Interpretation Against IS 383

Since the mean ACV (17.13 %) is **well below 30 %**, the aggregate is classified as **strong and suitable for wearing surfaces**. It is therefore **acceptable for use in all categories of plain and reinforced concrete**, including high-strength grades (M30 and above) as per IS 383 – 1970.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

The following table helps students quickly interpret any ACV result obtained in the lab. This is a **favourite viva question** in KTU lab examinations.

| Range of ACV (%) | Aggregate Quality | Recommended Application |
|---|---|---|
| 0 to 20 | **Very Strong** | High-grade concrete (M30+), wearing surfaces, bridge decks, runways |
| 20 to 30 | **Strong** | General RCC structures, building columns, beams, slabs |
| 30 to 45 | **Moderate** | Plain concrete, mass concrete, non-structural fills |
| Above 45 | **Weak / Unsuitable** | Reject the lot — do **not** use in concrete |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often confuse the "passing 2.36 mm" weight with the *retained* weight when calculating ACV. Always remember: the **fine material passing the sieve represents the crushed portion**, which is in the **numerator** of the formula. Mis-swapping numerator and denominator can produce a value > 100%, which is a clear sign of a logical error.

## 3.4 Derivation of Compressive Stress on Aggregate Bed

For the high-density physics involved, it is useful to compute the actual compressive stress experienced by the aggregate bed in the cylinder.

The cross-sectional area of the test cylinder is:

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times d^2 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times (115)^2
$$

$$
A \;=\; 0.7854 \times 13225 \;=\; 10386.89 \;\text{mm}^2
$$

The applied force from the 40-tonne load is:

$$
F \;=\; 40 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \;=\; 392400 \;\text{N}
$$

The compressive stress on the aggregate bed is therefore:

$$
\sigma \;=\; \frac{F}{A} \;=\; \frac{392400}{10386.89} \;\approx\; 37.78 \;\text{N/mm}^2 \;\approx\; 37.78 \;\text{MPa}
$$

This stress of approximately **37.78 MPa** is what each aggregate particle experiences. Since this is significantly higher than the typical **uniaxial compressive strength of weak aggregates (15–30 MPa)**, the test is sensitive enough to detect even minor variations in aggregate quality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete ACV Test Procedure

The following block-level functional flowchart captures every step of the test in a clean, easily reproducible diagram for inclusion in the KTU lab record book.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["START: Receive Coarse Aggregate Sample"] --> A2["Sieve through 12.5 mm IS Sieve"]
    A2 --> A3["Discard Retained on 12.5 mm"]
    A3 --> A4["Sieve Remainder through 10 mm IS Sieve"]
    A4 --> A5["Discard Fines passing 10 mm"]
    A5 --> A6["Collect Fraction Retained on 10 mm"]
    A6 --> A7["Oven Dry at 100 to 110 deg C for 4 hours"]
    A7 --> A8["Cool to Room Temperature in Desiccator"]
    A8 --> B1["Place Cylinder on Flat Base"]
    B1 --> B2["Fill Layer 1: Tamping 25 times"]
    B2 --> B3["Fill Layer 2: Tamping 25 times"]
    B3 --> B4["Fill Layer 3: Tamping 25 times"]
    B4 --> B5["Level Top Surface and Weigh Cylinder plus Aggregate"]
    B5 --> B6["Compute W1: Sample Weight"]
    B6 --> C1["Place Plunger Centrally on Aggregate"]
    C1 --> C2["Position Assembly in Compression Testing Machine"]
    C2 --> C3["Apply Load at 4 tonnes per minute"]
    C3 --> C4["Reach Peak Load of 40 tonnes"]
    C4 --> C5["Hold Load for Total 10 minutes"]
    C5 --> C6["Release Load and Remove Cylinder"]
    C6 --> D1["Empty Crushed Aggregate onto Clean Tray"]
    D1 --> D2["Sieve Entire Material through 2.36 mm IS Sieve"]
    D2 --> D3["Weigh Fines Passing 2.36 mm as W2"]
    D3 --> E1["Compute ACV equals W2 divided by W1 times 100"]
    E1 --> E2["Repeat for Trial 2 on Fresh Sample"]
    E2 --> E3["Compute Mean ACV"]
    E3 --> E4["Compare with IS 383 Limits"]
    E4 --> E5["Report Result and Interpretation"]
    E5 --> F1["END OF TEST"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Test Apparatus Architecture

The following diagram maps the functional components of the ACV test apparatus and their inter-relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Oven 100 to 110 deg C"] --> P2["Prepared Test Sample 10 to 12.5 mm"]
    P2 --> P3["Steel Cylinder 115 mm x 180 mm"]
    P3 --> P4["Tamping Rod 16 mm dia"]
    P4 --> P3
    P3 --> P5["Weighing Balance 1 g accuracy"]
    P5 --> P6["W1 Sample Weight in g"]
    P3 --> P7["Steel Plunger"]
    P7 --> P8["Compression Testing Machine 40 t capacity"]
    P8 --> P9["Applied Load 40 tonnes at 4 t per min"]
    P9 --> P10["Crushed Aggregate Output"]
    P10 --> P11["2.36 mm IS Sieve"]
    P11 --> P12["Fines W2 in g"]
    P12 --> P13["ACV Computation Unit"]
    P6 --> P13
    P13 --> P14["Final ACV percent Value"]
    P14 --> P15["Comparison with IS 383 Acceptance"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of Test Cylinder Assembly

For a lab record, the apparatus can be visualised as a vertical column with the following stacking order (top to bottom):

| Position (Top → Bottom) | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loading Platen of CTM | Applies uniform vertical compressive force |
| 2 | Steel Plunger | Transmits CTM load to aggregate |
| 3 | Aggregate Bed (3 tamped layers) | The test specimen |
| 4 | Cylinder Wall (115 mm dia) | Confines aggregate laterally |
| 5 | Detachable Steel Base Plate | Seals the cylinder bottom |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the cylinder made of hardened steel?** The cylinder must be **rigid** so that nearly all the energy from the CTM goes into **crushing the aggregate**, not into deforming the cylinder. Any cylinder wall deformation would absorb energy and produce a falsely *low* ACV.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

Define **Aggregate Crushing Value**. State the relevant IS code and the maximum permissible ACV for aggregates used in concrete as per IS 383.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)** of a coarse aggregate is defined as the **percentage of fines (passing 2.36 mm IS Sieve) produced** when a dry aggregate sample (passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm) is subjected to a **standard compressive load of 40 tonnes** applied at a uniform rate of 4 tonnes per minute in a cylindrical steel mould, as per **IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963**. (1½ Marks)
>
> The formula is $\text{ACV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$, where $W_1$ is the original dry sample weight and $W_2$ is the weight of the fraction passing the 2.36 mm sieve. (1 Mark)
>
> As per **IS 383 – 1970**, the ACV of coarse aggregate for concrete shall not exceed **45%** for general use, while for **wearing surfaces**, a lower limit of **30%** is preferred. (½ Mark)

---

### Question 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

List the **apparatus required** to conduct the Aggregate Crushing Value test and state the dimensions of the test cylinder and tamping rod.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The apparatus required for the ACV test is: (1 Mark)
>
> 1. **Steel Cylindrical Measure** of internal diameter **115 mm** and internal depth **180 mm**, fitted with a detachable base plate.
> 2. **Tamping Rod** of diameter **16 mm**, length **450–600 mm**, with one end rounded to a hemispherical tip.
> 3. **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)** capable of applying **40 tonnes** of load at a uniform rate of **4 tonnes/minute**.
> 4. **IS Sieves** of sizes **12.5 mm, 10 mm, and 2.36 mm**.
> 5. **Weighing Balance** with an accuracy of **1 g**.
> 6. **Oven** maintained at **100–110 °C** for drying the sample.
>
> (Cylinder dimensions: 115 mm dia × 180 mm depth; Tamping rod: 16 mm dia — 1 Mark for correctly stating dimensions; Remaining 1 Mark for the rest of the apparatus list.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Under the 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, apparatus, and procedure** for determining the Aggregate Crushing Value of a coarse aggregate sample as per IS 2386 (Part IV). (7 Marks)

**(b)** During an ACV test, the following observations were recorded:

| Trial | Empty Cylinder Weight | Cylinder + Aggregate (Before Test) | Weight of Fines Passing 2.36 mm |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4825 g | 7350 g | 415 g |
| 2 | 4825 g | 7370 g | 438 g |

Calculate the **mean Aggregate Crushing Value** and comment on the **suitability of the aggregate** for use in concrete. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a) (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
The Aggregate Crushing Value test measures the **mechanical resistance of coarse aggregate** to gradual compressive loading. The aggregate is subjected to a standardized compressive stress of approximately **37.78 MPa** (40 tonnes load) and the resulting proportion of crushed fines is used as an empirical index of strength. The test follows **IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963**.

**Apparatus (2 Marks):**
- Steel cylindrical measure (115 mm internal diameter, 180 mm height) with detachable base.
- Steel plunger fitting snugly inside the cylinder.
- Tamping rod (16 mm diameter, hemispherical tip).
- Compression Testing Machine (40-tonne capacity, with controlled loading rate).
- IS Sieves: 12.5 mm, 10 mm, 2.36 mm.
- Weighing balance (1 g accuracy) and drying oven (100–110 °C).

**Procedure (3 Marks):**
1. Prepare a sample passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm IS sieves; oven-dry at 100–110 °C for 4 hours and cool.
2. Fill the cylinder in **3 layers**, tamping each layer **25 times** with the 16 mm rod.
3. Level the top surface, weigh the cylinder + aggregate; subtract empty cylinder weight to obtain $W_1$.
4. Place the plunger on the aggregate; position the assembly in the CTM.
5. Apply load at **4 tonnes/minute** uniformly up to **40 tonnes**; hold for 10 minutes.
6. Release the load; remove the aggregate; sieve through **2.36 mm IS Sieve**.
7. Weigh the fines passing 2.36 mm as $W_2$.
8. Compute $\text{ACV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ and repeat for a second trial.

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b) (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $W_1$ for Trial 1:**

$$
W_1^{(1)} \;=\; 7350 \;-\; 4825 \;=\; 2525 \;\text{g}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ACV for Trial 1:**

$$
\text{ACV}_1 \;=\; \frac{415}{2525} \times 100 \;=\; 16.44 \,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $W_1$ for Trial 2:**

$$
W_1^{(2)} \;=\; 7370 \;-\; 4825 \;=\; 2545 \;\text{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ACV for Trial 2:**

$$
\text{ACV}_2 \;=\; \frac{438}{2545} \times 100 \;=\; 17.21 \,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Mean ACV:**

$$
\text{Mean ACV} \;=\; \frac{16.44 + 17.21}{2} \;=\; 16.83 \,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Suitability Comment (1 Mark):**

Since the mean ACV of **16.83 %** is well below the **30 %** limit specified for wearing surfaces and far below the **45 %** limit for general concrete (IS 383), the aggregate is classified as **strong and suitable** for all categories of concrete, including **high-grade structural concrete (M30+)** and **wearing surfaces**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**(a)** Describe the **limitations of the Aggregate Crushing Value test** and compare it with the **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)** test. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A coarse aggregate sample gave an ACV of **24.5 %**. Explain whether this aggregate is suitable for:
   1. M25 grade RCC construction.
   2. Bituminous concrete wearing course in a national highway.
   3. Plain concrete foundation in a residential building. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B(a) (7 Marks):**

**Limitations of ACV Test (3 Marks):**
1. It is an **empirical** test, not a fundamental material property; the value is valid only under the strict IS 2386 protocol.
2. The test is **destructive**, so the same sample cannot be retested.
3. It is sensitive to **particle shape**: flaky and elongated particles may produce misleadingly high ACV.
4. The test is **unconfined** in the lateral direction; it does not fully simulate the multi-axial stress state in real concrete.
5. Aggregate with **micro-cracks or weathered surfaces** may show high ACV even if its bulk compressive strength is acceptable.

**Comparison with AIV Test (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | ACV Test | AIV Test |
|---|---|---|
| Loading Mode | Gradual compression (static) | Sudden impact (dynamic) |
| Reference Standard | IS 2386 (Part IV) | IS 2386 (Part IV) |
| Total Load | 40 tonnes | 15 kg hammer × 15 blows (fall 380 mm) |
| Acceptance Limit (general) | ≤ 45 % | ≤ 45 % (≤ 30 % for wearing) |
| Simulates | Static loading like dead load, columns | Impact loading like traffic, hammers |
| Apparatus | CTM | Impact testing machine |
| Cylinder/Specimen | 115 × 180 mm cylinder | 115 × 50 mm cup |
| Fines Fraction Sieve | 2.36 mm | 2.36 mm |

The two tests are **complementary**: ACV covers static compression scenarios, AIV covers dynamic impact scenarios. For critical applications like road base or wearing courses, both tests are usually performed.

---

**Model Solution for Question B(b) (7 Marks):**

Given **ACV = 24.5 %**, the aggregate falls into the "Strong" category (20–30 %).

**(i) M25 grade RCC construction (2 Marks):**
Yes, **suitable**. The ACV of 24.5 % is well below the 30 % limit and far below the 45 % general limit. The aggregate can be used for **M25 grade RCC** with confidence in mechanical performance.

**(ii) Bituminous concrete wearing course in a national highway (3 Marks):**
**Marginally suitable** but **not preferred**. For a **national highway wearing course**, the preferred limit is **ACV ≤ 30 %** (with the conservative design criterion being ≤ 25 %). At 24.5 %, the aggregate meets the upper limit but is on the higher side. It is recommended to either (a) **blend** this aggregate with a stronger one (lower ACV), or (b) **conduct the Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) test** to confirm its resistance to dynamic traffic loading. If AIV is also within limits, the blend can be used; otherwise, reject for wearing course application.

**(iii) Plain concrete foundation in a residential building (2 Marks):**
**Suitable**. The ACV of 24.5 % is comfortably below the 45 % general limit for concrete (non-wearing). Plain concrete foundations in residential buildings carry relatively low static loads, and the aggregate will perform adequately.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong formula placement:** Forgetting that $W_2$ (the fines) goes in the **numerator**. Marks deducted = 2–3.
> 2. **Confusing the load rate with the load duration:** Writing "4 minutes" instead of "4 tonnes per minute". Marks deducted = 1.
> 3. **No second trial:** Reporting only one ACV value and skipping the mean calculation. The standard mandates duplicate tests. Marks deducted = 1.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the IS code:** Always cite **IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963** for the test method and **IS 383 – 1970** for the acceptance limit. Marks deducted = ½ to 1.
> 5. **No drying step mentioned:** Examiners check whether the student knows that the sample must be oven-dried. Marks deducted = 1.
> 6. **Reporting ACV without interpretation:** The final sentence must compare the result against **30%** (wearing) and **45%** (general) limits. Marks deducted = 1–2.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Bullet-Point Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- **Full name:** Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) Test.
- **Reference standard for test method:** IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963.
- **Reference standard for acceptance:** IS 383 – 1970.
- **Aggregate size used:** Passing 12.5 mm, retained on 10 mm IS Sieve.
- **Sample must be oven-dried** at 100–110 °C for 4 hours and cooled.
- **Cylinder dimensions:** 115 mm internal diameter × 180 mm internal depth.
- **Tamping rod:** 16 mm diameter, with rounded end.
- **Number of layers:** 3; **Tamping strokes per layer:** 25.
- **Total applied load:** 40 tonnes; **Loading rate:** 4 tonnes/minute; **Total duration:** 10 minutes.
- **Computed stress on aggregate bed:** ≈ 37.78 MPa.
- **Final sieving aperture:** 2.36 mm IS Sieve.
- **Formula:** $\text{ACV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ (with $W_1$ = original dry weight, $W_2$ = weight of fines passing 2.36 mm).
- **Acceptance limit (general concrete):** ACV ≤ 45 %.
- **Acceptance limit (wearing surfaces):** ACV ≤ 30 %.
- **Lower ACV ⇒ Stronger aggregate.**
- **Always report mean of two trials** with two decimal places.
- **ACV test is complementary to AIV test:** ACV covers static load; AIV covers impact load.
- **Apparatus set:** Cylinder + plunger + tamping rod + CTM + IS sieves + balance + oven.
- **Major pitfalls:** Wrong numerator in formula, confusing rate vs. duration, missing the drying step, omitting IS code references, no interpretation of result.
- **Engineering significance:** Determines the suitability of coarse aggregate for structural concrete, pavements, bridge decks, and runways.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tests on Fresh Concrete: Measurement of Workability by Slump Cone Test and Compacting Factor Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL407 - MATERIAL TESTING LAB II
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tests on Fresh Concrete: Measurement of Workability by Slump Cone Test and Compacting Factor Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Workability

According to the **KTU 2024 PCCVL407 syllabus** and the Indian Standard code **IS 6461 (Part VII)**, **Workability** of fresh concrete is defined as:

> *"The property of fresh concrete which determines the ease and homogeneity with which it can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished."*

In simpler, more intuitive terms — **Workability is a measure of "how easily wet concrete flows"** without losing its homogeneity (uniformity of mix).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> Workability is the amount of useful internal work necessary to produce 100% compaction of fresh concrete, expressed in terms of energy consumed per unit volume of concrete.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Toothpaste Test

Imagine you have three tubes of paste:

- **Tube A (Stiff Mix):** Requires enormous squeezing effort to push out. The paste comes out in chunks — **Low Workability**.
- **Tube B (Medium Mix):** Flows out smoothly with moderate hand pressure, holds its shape on the brush — **Medium Workability** (ideal for most construction).
- **Tube C (Watery Mix):** Pours out like a liquid the moment you open the cap — segregates into water and solids — **Too High Workability** (unstable).

**Fresh concrete behaves exactly like this.** The Slump Cone Test and Compacting Factor Test are simply two standardized "squeeze tests" used by civil engineers to quantify this property.

---

### 1.3 The Four Sub-Properties of Workability

Workability is a composite property consisting of four interrelated components:

1. **Consistency** – Ability of fresh concrete to flow (measured directly by Slump Test).
2. **Mobility** – Ease of movement of concrete under external effort.
3. **Compactibility** – Ease with which air voids are removed during compaction (measured by Compacting Factor Test).
4. **Finishability** – Ability of the surface to be finished smooth after compaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Different Tests?**
> - The **Slump Cone Test** is suitable for **medium to high workability** concrete (slump > 50 mm).
> - The **Compacting Factor Test** is preferred for **low to medium workability** concrete (stiff mixes) where the slump cone test is insensitive (slump value is too small to measure reliably).

---

### 1.4 Governing Standards

| Standard Code | Title |
|---------------|-------|
| **IS 1199 : 1959** (Reaffirmed 2018) | Methods of Sampling and Analysis of Fresh Concrete |
| **IS 6461 (Part VII)** | Glossary of Terms Relating to Fresh Concrete |
| **ASTM C143 / C143M** | Standard Test Method for Slump of Hydraulic-Cement Concrete |
| **BS 1881 : Part 102** | Method for Determination of Slump |
| **BS 1881 : Part 103** | Method for Determination of Compacting Factor |

---

### 1.5 IS 456 : 2000 Recommended Slump Values (High-Yield for KTU)

| Placing Conditions | Slump (mm) |
|--------------------|------------|
| Blinding concrete (lean concrete) | **20 – 40** |
| Mass concrete (foundations, footings) | **25 – 50** |
| RCC beams, slabs, columns (lightly reinforced) | **40 – 75** |
| RCC slabs, beams, columns (heavily reinforced) | **75 – 100** |
| Pumped concrete | **100 – 150** |
| Tremie concrete (underwater) | **150 – 200** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the 25–50 mm and 75–100 mm values — these are the most frequently asked in KTU viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 TEST 1 — SLUMP CONE TEST (IS 1199 : 1959)

#### 2.1.1 Apparatus Required

- **Slump Cone (Mould):** A truncated hollow frustum of a cone with internal dimensions:
  - Bottom diameter: **200 mm**
  - Top diameter: **100 mm**
  - Height: **300 mm**
  - Wall thickness: **1.6 mm** minimum (sheet metal)
- **Tamping Rod:** Straight steel rod of **16 mm diameter**, **600 mm long**, with one end bullet-nosed.
- **Base Plate:** Smooth, flat, non-absorbent rigid surface (steel/metal) of minimum **500 × 500 mm**.
- **Measuring Scale:** Graduated in **mm**.
- **Trowel, Scoop, Stopwatch.**

#### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Procedure

1. **Clean & Dampen:** Wipe the slump cone internally and place it on the smooth horizontal base plate. The cone is held firmly in place (usually by foot clamps or by standing on the foot-rests).
2. **Layer 1 Filling:** Fill the cone with freshly mixed concrete in **4 equal layers** (each ~75 mm thick) for higher accuracy. *Note: Many textbooks still mention 3 layers per ACI code — KTU accepts both, but IS specifies **4 layers** for laboratory accuracy.*
3. **Compaction of Each Layer:** Rod each layer uniformly with **25 strokes** of the tamping rod, distributed evenly over the cross-section. The rod must penetrate only into the layer being compacted (not into the layer below).
4. **Top Striking:** After the 4th layer is rodded and leveled, strike off the surface with a trowel in a rolling motion so the cone is exactly full.
5. **Lifting:** Clean any spilled concrete from the base plate. Carefully lift the cone vertically upward in **3 to 7 seconds** — a steady, smooth, single upward motion with no twisting or lateral movement.
6. **Slump Measurement:** Place the cone (inverted) beside the slumped concrete. Lay the tamping rod horizontally across the inverted cone, so it overhangs over the slumped concrete. Measure the **vertical difference between the top of the cone (rod level) and the original displaced center of the concrete** to the nearest **5 mm**. This is the **Slump Value**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The entire operation from start of filling to removal of the mould must be completed within 2 minutes.**

#### 2.1.3 Three Types of Slump (Diagnostic)

| Slump Type | Shape | Inference | Workability |
|------------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **True Slump** | Subsides uniformly, retains cone shape | Cohesive, well-proportioned mix | Acceptable |
| **Shear Slump** | Top portion shears off and slides sideways | Lack of cohesion / harsh mix | Reject the test — repeat |
| **Collapse Slump** | Concrete collapses completely | Over-wet, segregation likely | Reject — high water content |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** If a shear slump is observed, the test must be **discarded and repeated** with a fresh sample. Do not report the numerical value of a shear slump.

#### 2.1.4 Limitations of Slump Test

- **Only suitable for plastic concrete** (slump between **25 mm to 175 mm**).
- **Not suitable for very stiff mixes** (< 25 mm) → use Vee-Bee Consistometer or Compacting Factor.
- **Not suitable for very wet mixes** (> 175 mm) → segregation makes measurement meaningless.
- Result is **operator-sensitive** (depends on lifting speed, filling, rodding).

---

### 2.2 TEST 2 — COMPACTING FACTOR TEST (IS 1199 : 1959)

#### 2.2.1 Background

Developed by **Glanville, Collins, and Matthews (1947)** in the UK. It is more sensitive than the slump test for **stiff / low-workability mixes**.

#### 2.2.2 Apparatus — The Compacting Factor Apparatus

The apparatus consists of **two conical hoppers** placed above a **cylindrical mould**, all with hinged trap doors:

- **Upper Hopper (Hopper A):** Top diameter **250 mm**, bottom opening **150 × 150 mm**.
- **Lower Hopper (Hopper B):** Top opening **230 × 230 mm**, bottom opening **150 × 150 mm**.
- **Cylindrical Mould (Mould C):** Internal diameter **150 mm**, height **300 mm**.

All internal surfaces must be **smooth, clean, and free from projections**.

#### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure

1. **Sample Preparation:** Obtain a representative fresh concrete sample of mass approximately **8 kg**.
2. **Place Sample in Upper Hopper (A):** With trap door of A closed, gently place the concrete in Hopper A using a scoop and trowel. **Do not rod or compact** — let the concrete fall freely.
3. **Release into Lower Hopper (B):** Open the trap door of A after **2 minutes**. The concrete falls under gravity into Hopper B.
4. **Release into Cylinder (C):** Immediately open the trap door of B. The concrete falls into the cylinder C, **overfilling** it (forming a heap).
5. **Strike Off:** Cut off the overflowing concrete flush with the top of cylinder C using a trowel. Measure the mass of concrete in C — this is the **Partially Compacted Weight ($W_1$)**.
6. **Fully Compacted Weight ($W_2$):** Refill cylinder C with the *same* concrete in **4 layers**, each rodded with **25 strokes** of the tamping rod, then strike off flush and weigh → this is the **Fully Compacted Weight ($W_2$)**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value symbol in tables below (per KTU LaTeX isolation rule).**

| Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|----------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| **Slump Value** | $S = H_m - H_s$ | mm | $H_m$ = mould height (300 mm); $H_s$ = subsided height |
| **Compacting Factor (CF)** | $CF = \dfrac{W_1}{W_2}$ | Dimensionless (decimal) | $W_1$ = partially compacted mass; $W_2$ = fully compacted mass |
| **Density of Compacted Concrete** | $\rho = \dfrac{W_2}{V_{cyl}}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $V_{cyl} = \pi r^2 h = \pi (0.075)^2 (0.3) = 5.30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$ |
| **Workability Range (CF)** | $0.70 \;\vert\; \text{to} \;\vert\; 0.95$ | — | Below 0.70 → very stiff; Above 0.95 → flowing |
| **Workability Range (Slump)** | $25 \;\vert\; \text{to} \;\vert\; 175$ | mm | Below 25 → use CF; Above 175 → segregation risk |
| **Yield of Concrete** | $Y = \dfrac{V_{concrete\ produced}}{V_{cement} + V_{sand} + V_{agg} + V_{water}}$ | Dimensionless | Should approach 1.0 |
| **Air Content (approx)** | $A\% = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_2} \times 100$ | % | Empirical relation for plastic mixes |

#### 2.3.1 IS 1199 Compactness Classification

| Compacting Factor | Workability Category |
|-------------------|----------------------|
| **0.85 – 0.95** | **High** workability |
| **0.70 – 0.85** | **Medium** workability |
| **0.60 – 0.70** | **Low** workability |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Favourite Question:** *"What is the significance of the factor 0.92 in compacting factor?"*
> Answer: A CF of 0.92 is generally considered the **ideal value** for ordinary structural concrete with a water-cement ratio of about 0.55–0.60.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control at RMC Plants:** Every batch of Ready-Mix Concrete is slump-tested before dispatch to ensure it can be placed on site without delays.
- **Pumpability Check:** High-rise pumping requires slump ≥ 100 mm and CF ≥ 0.92.
- **Compliance Verification:** IS 456 : 2000 mandates slump checks before pouring any structural element.
- **Comparisons Across Sites:** CF is preferred for stiffer mixes used in **roads (rigid pavements), dam concrete, and foundations** where slump would be too small to measure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Compacting Factor Equation

#### 3.1.1 Concept Setup

The compacting factor is defined as the **ratio of the density of partially compacted concrete to the density of fully compacted concrete**, both occupying the same volume (the cylinder C of volume $V$).

By definition of density:

$$
\rho_{\text{partial}} = \frac{W_1}{V}
$$

$$
\rho_{\text{full}} = \frac{W_2}{V}
$$

#### 3.1.2 Forming the Ratio

Dividing $\rho_{\text{partial}}$ by $\rho_{\text{full}}$:

$$
CF = \frac{\rho_{\text{partial}}}{\rho_{\text{full}}} = \frac{\dfrac{W_1}{V}}{\dfrac{W_2}{V}}
$$

The volume $V$ cancels out (since the **same cylinder** is used in both measurements):

$$
CF = \frac{W_1}{W_2}
$$

This is the **dimensionless** compacting factor, which is **independent of the actual volume** of the cylinder, making it a true material property.

#### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example — Compacting Factor

**Given:**
- Mass of partially compacted concrete in cylinder C: $W_1 = 12.40\ \text{kg}$
- Mass of fully compacted concrete in cylinder C: $W_2 = 13.75\ \text{kg}$
- Cylinder volume: $V = 5.30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$

**Step 1: Compute Compacted Density**

$$
\rho_{\text{full}} = \frac{W_2}{V} = \frac{13.75\ \text{kg}}{5.30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3} = 2594.34\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Compute Compacting Factor**

$$
CF = \frac{W_1}{W_2} = \frac{12.40}{13.75} = 0.9018
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

A CF of **0.902** falls in the range **0.85 – 0.95**, indicating **High Workability** — suitable for **RCC slabs, columns, and beams with congested reinforcement**.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Slump Cone Test

**Given:**
- Slump cone height: $H_m = 300\ \text{mm}$
- Subsided concrete height (measured after lifting): $H_s = 215\ \text{mm}$

**Step 1: Compute Slump**

$$
S = H_m - H_s = 300 - 215 = 85\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Classification (per IS 456)**

A slump of **85 mm** falls in the **75 – 100 mm** range → **Heavily Reinforced Sections** (columns, deep beams, slab-column junctions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Single blank line rule obeyed above and below every $$ block.**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Test Selection

| Concrete Mix Description | Slump (estimated, mm) | Recommended Test | Reason |
|--------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|--------|
| Dry / Stiff (road base, mass concrete) | < 25 | **Compacting Factor** | Slump too small to measure accurately |
| Medium (ordinary RCC) | 25 – 75 | Either (Slump preferred) | Slump is faster and field-portable |
| Plastic (heavily reinforced, pumped) | 75 – 175 | Slump Cone | CF less sensitive in this range |
| Highly fluid (SCC, tremie) | > 175 | Slump Flow Test (not in syllabus) | Cone not suitable — use Abram's cone spread |

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Air-Void Relation from Compaction Data

The fractional air-void volume after free fall (no compaction) can be approximated as:

$$
A_v = 1 - CF
$$

because $CF$ represents the **fraction of theoretical solid volume filled** by free-falling concrete. Air-void percentage is therefore:

$$
A\% = (1 - CF) \times 100
$$

**Verification with the worked example:**

$$
A\% = (1 - 0.9018) \times 100 = 9.82\%
$$

This **~10% entrapped air** is reasonable for fresh concrete without vibration, justifying why mechanical compaction is mandatory for structural elements.

---

### 3.5 Cumulative Mass–Volume Calculation for Yield

**Given batch quantities per $\text{m}^3$ of concrete:**
- Cement: $300\ \text{kg}$ (specific gravity $G_c = 3.15$)
- Sand: $660\ \text{kg}$ ($G_s = 2.65$)
- Coarse Aggregate: $1230\ \text{kg}$ ($G_a = 2.70$)
- Water: $150\ \text{kg}$ ($G_w = 1.00$)

$$
V_c = \frac{300}{3.15 \times 1000} = 0.0952\ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{660}{2.65 \times 1000} = 0.2491\ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_a = \frac{1230}{2.70 \times 1000} = 0.4556\ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_w = \frac{150}{1.00 \times 1000} = 0.1500\ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_{\text{total solids+liquid}} = 0.0952 + 0.2491 + 0.4556 + 0.1500 = 0.9499\ \text{m}^3
$$

The deficit $\left(1 - 0.9499 = 0.0501\ \text{m}^3\right)$ represents **entrapped air + yield loss**, which is consistent with the ~5–10% expected for normal concrete.

---

### 3.6 Sensitivity / Error Analysis

For the compacting factor formula, propagating errors:

$$
\frac{\Delta(CF)}{CF} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\Delta W_1}{W_1}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\Delta W_2}{W_2}\right)^2}
$$

For a typical electronic balance with $\Delta W = \pm 0.01\ \text{kg}$:

$$
\frac{\Delta(CF)}{CF} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{0.01}{12.40}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{0.01}{13.75}\right)^2} = \sqrt{6.50 \times 10^{-7} + 5.29 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.00109
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta(CF) = 0.00109 \times 0.9018 \approx 0.0010
$$

**Result:** $CF = 0.902 \pm 0.001$ — highly reliable measurement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Slump Cone Test — Apparatus Schematic (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Concrete Sample] --> B[Clean & Dampen Slump Cone]
    B --> C[Place Cone on Base Plate]
    C --> D[Fill Layer 1 - 25 strokes]
    D --> E[Fill Layer 2 - 25 strokes]
    E --> F[Fill Layer 3 - 25 strokes]
    F --> G[Fill Layer 4 - 25 strokes]
    G --> H[Strike Off Top with Trowel]
    H --> I[Lift Cone Vertically in 3-7 sec]
    I --> J{Slump Type?}
    J -->|True Slump| K[Measure Slump = Hm - Hs]
    J -->|Shear Slump| L[Discard - Repeat Test]
    J -->|Collapse Slump| M[Discard - Mix Too Wet]
    K --> N[Record Value to nearest 5 mm]
    L --> O[Take Fresh Sample]
    M --> O
    O --> C
```

### 4.2 Slump Cone Geometry (Mermaid Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Diameter 100 mm] --> B[Truncated Frustum Cone]
    B --> C[Bottom Diameter 200 mm]
    B --> D[Height 300 mm]
    B --> E[Wall Thickness 1.6 mm]
    F[Tamping Rod 16 mm dia 600 mm long] --> B
    G[Smooth Base Plate 500 x 500 mm] --> B
```

### 4.3 Compacting Factor Test — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[8 kg Fresh Concrete Sample] --> B[Place in Upper Hopper A]
    B --> C[Wait 2 minutes]
    C --> D[Open Trap Door of A]
    D --> E[Concrete falls into Lower Hopper B]
    E --> F[Immediately Open Trap Door of B]
    F --> G[Concrete falls into Cylinder C]
    G --> H[Strike Off Flush with Trowel]
    H --> I[Weigh Cylinder = W1 Partial]
    I --> J[Refill Cylinder in 4 Layers]
    J --> K[Rod Each Layer 25 times]
    K --> L[Strike Off Flush]
    L --> M[Weigh Cylinder = W2 Full]
    M --> N[Compute CF = W1 / W2]
    N --> O{CF Value?}
    O -->|0.85 - 0.95| P[High Workability]
    O -->|0.70 - 0.85| Q[Medium Workability]
    O -->|0.60 - 0.70| R[Low Workability]
```

### 4.4 Compacting Factor Apparatus Geometry (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Upper Hopper A]
        A1[Top 250 mm]
        A2[Bottom 150 x 150 mm]
    end
    subgraph S2[Lower Hopper B]
        B1[Top 230 x 230 mm]
        B2[Bottom 150 x 150 mm]
    end
    subgraph S3[Cylinder C]
        C1[Diameter 150 mm]
        C2[Height 300 mm]
        C3[Volume 5.30 x 10 power -3 m cube]
    end
    A2 --> B1
    B2 --> C1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
```

### 4.5 Decision Flow — Which Test to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Concrete Sample] --> B{Visual Estimate}
    B -->|Stiff Mix| C[Compacting Factor Test]
    B -->|Medium Mix| D{Site Facility}
    D -->|Lab with Hopper| E[Compacting Factor Test]
    D -->|Field Quick Check| F[Slump Cone Test]
    B -->|Plastic Mix| F
    C --> G[Compute CF]
    F --> H[Compute Slump in mm]
    G --> I[Compare with IS 1199]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Accept or Reject Batch]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define workability of fresh concrete and list any four factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer:**

Workability is the property of fresh concrete that determines the ease with which it can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation. It is a composite property reflecting consistency, mobility, compactibility, and finishability.

Four factors affecting workability:

1. **Water Content** – Increasing water increases workability but reduces strength.
2. **Aggregate Properties** – Shape (rounded = better), size (larger = better), texture (smooth = better), grading.
3. **Cement Content & Type** – More cement paste → better workability.
4. **Admixtures** – Plasticizers/superplasticizers increase workability without extra water.
5. *Temperature of concrete* and *Time of transport* are also valid additional factors.

> *Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for factors (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks).*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. What is meant by "True Slump" and "Shear Slump"? Which one is acceptable?**

**Model Answer:**

- **True Slump:** The fresh concrete subsides uniformly in a symmetrical manner, retaining a recognizable cone shape. It indicates a cohesive, well-proportioned mix. This is the **only acceptable** slump type.
- **Shear Slump:** The top half of the cone shears off and slides down one side, indicating a harsh, non-cohesive mix lacking fine material. This is **unacceptable** — the test must be discarded and repeated with a fresh sample.
- **Collapse Slump:** The entire concrete mass collapses into a heap — indicates excessive water content; also unacceptable.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2]**

#### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Slump Cone Test** for measuring workability of fresh concrete. Mention the IS code, apparatus, procedure, and the different types of slump observed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**IS Code:** IS 1199 : 1959 (Reaffirmed 2018).

**Apparatus:**
- Slump cone (truncated frustum): top 100 mm, bottom 200 mm, height 300 mm.
- Tamping rod: 16 mm dia, 600 mm long, bullet-nosed.
- Smooth non-absorbent base plate.
- Measuring scale (graduated in mm).
- Trowel and scoop.

**Procedure:**
1. Dampen the internal surface of the slump cone and place it firmly on the base plate.
2. Fill the cone with fresh concrete in **4 equal layers** (each ~75 mm thick).
3. Rod each layer with **25 strokes** of the tamping rod, distributing the strokes evenly. The rod must just penetrate into the layer being compacted.
4. Strike off the top surface with a trowel in a rolling motion.
5. Hold the cone firmly by its handles; remove any spilled concrete from the base.
6. Lift the cone vertically upward in a steady motion taking **3 to 7 seconds** without any lateral or twisting movement.
