## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A, Q1(a)]**
> **Q1.** *List any three IS codes applicable for testing of bricks and state the acceptance limit for water absorption of bricks.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **IS 1077 : 1992** – Specification for common burnt clay building bricks. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **IS 3495 (Parts 1–4)** – Methods of tests for burnt clay building bricks. *(1 mark)*
> 3. **IS 5454** – Methods of sampling of clay building bricks (used for selecting test specimens). *(0.5 mark)*
> 4. **Acceptance limit for water absorption:** Should **not exceed 20 %** of the dry weight of the brick when tested as per **IS 3495 (Part 2)**. *(0.5 mark)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B, Q1(c)]**
> **Q2.** *What is the difference between the compressive strength test and the transverse strength test? Why are different formulas used?*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Compressive strength test** (for **bricks**) applies a **uniform axial load** on the bed face; stress is load divided by the bed-face area, $\sigma_c = P / (L \times W)$. *(1 mark)*
> - **Transverse strength test** (for **floor and roof tiles**) applies a **central point load** on a tile simply supported at both ends; the failure is by **flexural bending**, governed by the bending formula $\sigma_f = 3 P L / (2 b d^{2})$. *(1 mark)*
> - The formulas differ because a brick fails by **axial compression** (uniform stress distribution) while a tile fails by **bending** (linearly varying stress, max at extreme fibre). *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q7]**

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
> *A batch of burnt clay bricks is supplied to a site. As the Junior Engineer in charge of quality control, design a complete testing programme for the batch. List the IS codes, the apparatus required, the procedural steps for each test, and the acceptance criteria. With neat sketches, explain the compressive strength test and water absorption test in detail. Calculate the compressive strength of a brick given: dimensions 230 × 110 × 70 mm, dry mass 2.85 kg, saturated mass 3.25 kg, and crushing load 380 kN. Comment on the quality class as per IS 1077.*

##### **Solution A:**

**Part (a) — Testing Programme (7 marks)**

1. **IS Codes to be referred:** IS 1077, IS 3495 (Parts 1–4), IS 5454 (sampling). *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Sampling:** Minimum 20 bricks per lot of similar type; choose at random. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Tests to be conducted:**
   - Visual & dimensional test *(0.5)*
   - Soundness test *(0.5)*
   - Efflorescence test *(0.5)*
   - Water absorption test *(1.0)*
   - Compressive strength test *(1.0)*
4. **Apparatus & Acceptance Criteria:** Tabulated below. *(3.0 marks)*

| Test | Apparatus | Acceptance Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensional | Vernier caliper | ± 3 mm (modular), ± 5 mm (non-modular) |
| Soundness | Hand striking | Clear ringing sound |
| Efflorescence | Tray, water | Nil / Slight (for exposed work) |
| Water Absorption | Oven, water tank, balance | ≤ 20 % |
| Compressive Strength | CTM (2000 kN), capping plate | ≥ 3.5 N/mm² (Class 3.5) |

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compressive strength calculation:**

$$
A_b = 230 \times 110 = 25\,300 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

$$
P_{\max} = 380 \;\text{kN} = 380\,000 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{380\,000}{25\,300} = 15.02 \;\text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

*(4 marks for the computation)*

**Step 2 — Water absorption calculation:**

$$
W_a = \dfrac{3.25 - 2.85}{2.85} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.40}{2.85} \times 100 = 14.04\,\%
$$

*(1.5 marks for the computation)*

**Step 3 — Quality classification & comment:**

Since $\sigma_c = 15.02 \;\text{N/mm}^{2}$, the brick falls in the **Class 15** category (between 10 and 20 N/mm² per IS 1077). Water absorption of 14.04 % is well within the 20 % limit. **The batch is ACCEPTED** for use in load-bearing masonry. *(1.5 marks for the comment)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
> *With the help of neat sketches, describe the procedure for determining the transverse strength of a cement concrete floor tile as per IS 1237. List the apparatus required. A floor tile of size 300 × 300 × 25 mm is tested on a span of 250 mm. The tile fails at a load of 6.8 kN. Calculate the transverse strength and state whether the tile conforms to IS 1237. Also describe the abrasion resistance test for the same tile.*

##### **Solution B:**

**Part (a) — Transverse Strength Test (7 marks)**

1. **IS Code:** IS 1237 : 2012 (cement concrete flooring tiles). *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Apparatus:** Universal Testing Machine (UTM) with a 3-roller bending fixture, two **25 mm diameter support rollers** (120 mm apart at the ends of the tile, span typically L = nominal length − 50 mm) and one **central loading roller** of the same diameter; Vernier caliper. *(1 mark)*
3. **Specimen preparation:** Tile must be **air-dried** for 24 h; measure length (L_nom), width (b) and thickness (d) to nearest 0.1 mm at **5 locations** (4 corners + centre). *(1 mark)*
4. **Procedure (with sketch):**
   - Place tile on the two **support rollers**, bed face up. *(0.5 mark)*
   - Apply load through the **central loading roller** perpendicular to the tile surface, increasing steadily at **20 N/s** until failure. *(0.5 mark)*
   - Record the **maximum load (P)** at failure. *(0.5 mark)*

*(Mermaid block in Section 4.3 represents the schematic.)* *(1 mark)*

5. **Computation & Verification:**

Given: $P = 6800$ N, $L = 250$ mm, $b = 300$ mm, $d = 25$ mm.

$$
\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}} = \dfrac{3 \times 6800 \times 250}{2 \times 300 \times 25^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_f = \dfrac{5\,100\,000}{375\,000} = 13.6 \;\text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

*(2 marks for the computation)*

Since **13.6 N/mm² ≥ 3 N/mm²** (IS 1237 minimum for general use), the **tile CONFORMS** to IS 1237. *(0.5 mark for the comment)*

**Part (b) — Abrasion Resistance Test (7 marks)**

1. **IS Code:** IS 1237 (Annex for abrasion); the apparatus is a **disc-type abrasion testing machine** (a flat steel disc rotating about a vertical axis). *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Apparatus:** Disc machine, abrasive powder (aluminium oxide of standard grit), caliper for thickness measurement, weighing balance. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Specimen preparation:** Cut a **100 × 100 mm** test piece from the tile (or use a small tile). Measure its initial **thickness t₁** and **mass m₁** at 4 marked points. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Procedure:**
   - Clamp the specimen on the disc, abrasive powder fed at **standard rate** between disc and specimen surface. *(0.5 mark)*
   - Rotate the disc for **1000 revolutions** under a **standard pressure** (≈ 0.06 N/mm²). *(0.5 mark)*
   - Remove, clean, measure final thickness **t₂** and final mass **m₂**. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Calculation & Acceptance:**

$$
\Delta t = t_1 - t_2
$$

$$
\text{Volume loss} = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{\rho_{tile}}
$$

*(0.5 mark for the formula presentation)*

- **Acceptance limit (IS 1237):**
  - For **general use:** $\Delta t \leq 2.0$ mm. *(0.5 mark)*
  - For **heavy-traffic use:** $\Delta t \leq 1.5$ mm. *(0.5 mark)*

6. **Inference:** A high abrasion loss means the tile is suitable only for low-traffic areas (e.g., bedrooms) and not for public corridors or commercial floors. *(1 mark)*

7. **Result Reporting:** The abrasion loss and class of use should be reported on the lab sheet along with the transverse strength value for the same batch. *(0.5 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the area for brick compression:** Computing area as the *net* area (excluding frog indentation) instead of the *gross* bed-face area $\to$ loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Missing the unit conversions:** kN to N, kg to g — students forget $1$ kN $= 1000$ N, costing 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Wrong formula in transverse strength:** Some students wrongly use $\sigma = P/A$ for tiles. Mark the formula as **flexural**, not compressive, to fetch full marks.
> 4. **Not quoting IS code numbers:** Always write *IS 3495 Part 1* or *IS 1237*, not "IS code for bricks".
> 5. **Omitting the sketch:** For 14-mark Part B questions, a labelled diagram of the bending test fixture is **mandatory** — losing 1–2 marks without it.
> 6. **Average vs. individual values:** Report the **average of 5 specimens** and the **minimum individual value**; both are required as per IS 5454 sampling rules.
> 7. **Forgetting the saturated surface-dry step:** In water absorption, after removal from the tank the specimen must be **wiped with a damp cloth** to surface-dry, not air-dried — a common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Brick** is tested as per **IS 3495 (Parts 1–4)**, governed by **IS 1077**. The most important tests are **compressive strength (≥ 3.5 N/mm²)** and **water absorption (≤ 20 %)**. Efflorescence is rated visually. Dimensional tolerance is **± 3 mm** for modular bricks.
- [ ] **Floor tile** is tested as per **IS 1237**. Key tests: **transverse strength (≥ 3 N/mm²)**, **water absorption (≤ 10 %)**, **abrasion (≤ 2.0 mm loss)**, **dimensional (± 1 mm)**.
- [ ] **Roof tile** (clay/terracing) is tested as per **IS 654 / IS 1464 / IS 2690**. Key tests: **transverse strength (≥ 3.5 N/mm²)**, **water absorption (≤ 10–12 %)**, **permeability (no drops in 2 h)**.
- [ ] Compressive strength formula: $\sigma_c = P / (L \times W)$ — use **gross bed-face area**.
- [ ] Water absorption formula: $W_a (\%) = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_1} \times 100$.
- [ ] Transverse (flexural) strength formula: $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}$ — derived from simple beam theory.
- [ ] Specimen counts (typical): **20 bricks** per lot, **6 floor tiles** per lot; report average and minimum individual value.
- [ ] Loading rates to remember: **14 N/mm²/min** for brick compression; **20 N/s** for tile bending.
- [ ] **Always quote the exact IS code** in your answers — e.g., *IS 3495 (Part 1) : 1992*.
- [ ] **Sketch is mandatory** in 14-mark Part B questions — draw the bending fixture (3-roller) and the capping arrangement for brick compression.
- [ ] **Conversion musts:** $1$ kN $= 1000$ N; $1$ N/mm² $= 1$ MPa; $1$ g/cm³ $= 1000$ kg/m³.
- [ ] **Efflorescence ratings** in ascending severity: **Nil → Slight → Moderate → Heavy → Serious**.
- [ ] **Soundness test** is qualitative (ringing sound) — record as a pass/fail observation in the lab report.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook for viva:**
> *"Brick → Compression + Absorption; Tile → Bending + Abrasion; Roof → Bending + Water-tightness."*
> If you remember only this sentence, you can derive the rest of the procedure during the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study on Non-Destructive Tests on Hardened Concrete (Rebound Hammer, Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity and Rebar Locator)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL407 - MATERIAL TESTING LAB II
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study on Non-Destructive Tests on Hardened Concrete (Rebound Hammer, Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity and Rebar Locator)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) of Hardened Concrete — The Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** of hardened concrete refers to a suite of *in-situ* evaluation techniques used to assess the **quality, strength, durability, uniformity, and integrity** of concrete in a structure **without causing any damage** to the member under test. The three principal NDT methods specified under **IS 13311 (Parts 1–3): 1992** and validated worldwide are: **(i) Rebound Hammer Test, (ii) Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity Test, and (iii) Rebar Locator (Cover Meter) Test**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this experiment falls under **Course Outcome CO2 / CO3** of PCCVL407, where the student must *demonstrate competence in assessing the in-service quality of concrete using standard IS-coded NDT methods*.

### Classification of NDT Methods

| Method | IS Code | Property Measured | Type of Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rebound Hammer | IS 13311 (Part 2) : 1992 | Surface Hardness | Qualitative Strength |
| Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity | IS 13311 (Part 1) : 1992 | Pulse Transit Time | Density/Uniformity/Modulus |
| Rebar Locator (Covermeter) | IS 13311 (Part 3) : 1992 / BS 1881-204 | Magnetic Permeability | Cover & Rebar Diameter |

---

## 1.2 The "Why" — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Doctor's Stethoscope for Concrete**
> Imagine a doctor checking a patient. The doctor never opens the body; instead, the doctor taps the chest (**rebound hammer — surface rebound**), uses an ultrasound probe (**ultrasonic pulse velocity — wave through body**), and uses an X-ray or MRI to locate bones (**rebar locator — finding steel inside concrete**). NDT works exactly this way — we "feel" the concrete's health from the outside without breaking it.

### Why NDT is Crucial in Civil Engineering
- **Drilled cores are destructive** — they weaken the structure.
- **Cubes cast on-site often misrepresent** the actual structural member (different compaction, curing, exposure).
- **NDT is fast, repeatable, and economical** for surveying large RCC frames, bridges, and dams.
- NDT helps in **acceptance testing**, **assessment of fire/chemical damage**, and **structural retrofitting** decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle:** NDT methods do *not* directly measure compressive strength. They measure an *index property* (hardness, velocity, magnetic response) that is *correlated* to strength through calibration curves established per **IS 13311**.

---

## 1.3 The Three NDT Methods at a Glance

### A. Rebound Hammer Test
The **Schmidt Rebound Hammer** measures the **rebound distance of a spring-loaded mass** striking the concrete surface. Harder surfaces yield higher rebound numbers.

- **Rebound Number (R)** = empirical hardness index (range **0–100**).
- Higher $R \Rightarrow$ higher surface hardness $\Rightarrow$ higher estimated compressive strength.
- Standards: **IS 13311 (Part 2) : 1992**, **IS 516 (Part 5/Sec 3)** for calibration.
- Hammer types: **N-type** (standard, impact energy = **2.207 N·m**), **L-type** (lightweight concrete), **M-type** (mass concrete).

### B. Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) Test
An **electro-acoustical transducer** sends high-frequency (**20 kHz–150 kHz**, usually **54 kHz**) pulses through the concrete; a second transducer receives them. Transit time $T$ is measured, and velocity $V = L / T$ is computed.

- **V > 4.5 km/s** ⇒ Excellent quality
- **3.5 – 4.5 km/s** ⇒ Good
- **3.0 – 3.5 km/s** ⇒ Medium / Doubtful
- **< 3.0 km/s** ⇒ Poor

### C. Rebar Locator (Cover Meter / Profometer)
An **electromagnetic device** that detects the location, orientation, and depth (cover) of reinforcing bars using **eddy current / magnetic flux perturbation** principles.

- Measures **concrete cover (mm)**.
- Estimates **rebar diameter (mm)**.
- Standards: **IS 13311 (Part 3) : 1992**, **BS 1881-204**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rebound Hammer — Working Principle

### Operating Sequence
1. The plunger is pressed firmly against the concrete surface — a spring is compressed.
2. At full compression, a **release catch** releases the hammer mass.
3. The hammer mass strikes the plunger; a portion of its kinetic energy is absorbed (by plastic deformation/indentation), and the remainder causes **rebound**.
4. A **slider** records the maximum rebound distance — this is the **Rebound Number $R$**.
5. $R$ is read on a graduated scale.

### Calibration & Anvil Check
- Before every test, the hammer must be tested on a **calibration anvil** supplied by the manufacturer.
- **Standard rebound value on the anvil = 80 ± 2** (for N-type hammer).
- If reading deviates, **mechanical adjustment** is needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO2/Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024):** The student must be able to *correlate the rebound number to an approximate cube compressive strength using the manufacturer's calibration chart or IS-provided empirical curve.*

### Rebound Hammer Correlation (IS 13311 Empirical Curve)
The relationship between $R$ and compressive strength $f_c$ is:

$$f_c = a \cdot R + b$$

where $a$ and $b$ are constants derived from calibration (typically supplied as a **graph** in the hammer kit). For example, for one standard N-type hammer with a horizontal downward orientation, an indicative curve gives:

| Rebound Number $R$ | Approx. $f_c$ (MPa) |
|---|---|
| 20 | 10 |
| 30 | 20 |
| 40 | 30 |
| 50 | 45 |

### Factors Affecting Rebound Number
- **Surface finish** — smooth, troweled surfaces give higher $R$.
- **Moisture content** — wet concrete gives *lower* $R$ (surface softens).
- **Carbonation** — gives *artificially high* $R$ (carbonated skin is harder).
- **Age of concrete** — $R$ increases with age.
- **Hammer orientation** (gravity effect on hammer mass):

| Test Direction | Correction |
|---|---|
| Horizontal | $R = R_{horiz}$ (no correction) |
| Downward (vertical down) | $R = R_{down} + \Delta_1$ |
| Upward (vertical up) | $R = R_{up} - \Delta_2$ |
| Inclined | Use chart |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply the **orientation correction factor** from the calibration chart. This typically costs 1–2 marks in ESE.

---

## 2.2 Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) Test — Working Principle

### Operating Sequence
1. A **transmitter (Tx)** generates an ultrasonic pulse.
2. The pulse travels through the concrete.
3. A **receiver (Rx)** detects the leading edge of the pulse.
4. The instrument measures **transit time $T$ (in $\mu s$)**.
5. Path length $L$ (distance between Tx and Rx) is measured physically.
6. **Pulse velocity $V$** is computed as $V = L / T$.

### The Three Test Configurations
```
DIRECT      : Tx and Rx on opposite faces  → BEST (full path)
SEMI-DIRECT  : Tx and Rx on adjacent faces → OK (corner elements)
INDIRECT    : Tx and Rx on same face       → WORST (surface waves dominate)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Direct transmission is the **most reliable** because the pulse travels through the bulk of the concrete. Indirect transmission uses surface waves and is sensitive to near-surface conditions.

### Velocity–Quality Criterion (IS 13311 Part 1)

| Pulse Velocity $V$ (km/s) | Concrete Quality |
|---|---|
| $> 4.5$ | Excellent |
| $3.5 - 4.5$ | Good |
| $3.0 - 3.5$ | Medium / Satisfactory |
| $2.0 - 3.0$ | Doubtful |
| $< 2.0$ | Poor |

### Factors Affecting UPV
- **Aggregate type and size** — dense aggregate increases $V$.
- **Moisture content** — wet concrete shows *higher* $V$ (water replaces air, density ↑).
- **Temperature** — extreme temperatures reduce $V$.
- **Reinforcing bars** — steel transmits pulses *much faster* (~5900 m/s) than concrete, giving artificially high $V$ when bars lie in the pulse path.
- **Path length** — must be ≥ **150 mm** for reliable reading.
- **Surface condition** — rough/coupled surfaces affect $T$.

---

## 2.3 Rebar Locator (Cover Meter) — Working Principle

### Electromagnetic Principle
A **probe containing two coils** — a primary (transmitter) and a secondary (receiver) — generates an alternating **magnetic field**. When a ferromagnetic object (rebar) is near, it perturbs the field, inducing **eddy currents** in the steel. These eddy currents change the **impedance of the primary coil**, which is read as a signal.

### What the Cover Meter Measures
- **Location of rebar** (X, Y coordinates) — by scanning the surface.
- **Concrete cover** — distance from surface to rebar.
- **Rebar diameter** — signal strength depends on bar diameter; for a known cover, diameter can be back-calculated.

### Typical Instrument Output
- A **meter needle or digital display** gives cover in mm.
- A **buzzer / LED** confirms bar location.
- Some instruments (e.g., **Profometer PM-630 AI**) give 2D/3D rebar maps.

### Calibration
- Calibration is done on a **reference block** with known bar size and known cover (usually supplied by manufacturer).
- Different bar sizes (6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25 mm) require **dial settings**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pulse Velocity | $V = \dfrac{L}{T}$ | m/s or km/s | $L$ = path length, $T$ = transit time |
| 2 | Dynamic Modulus of Elasticity (from UPV) | $E_d = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V^2 (1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}{1-\nu}$ | MPa | $\rho$ = density, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 3 | Simplified $E_d$ (most cited) | $E_d = \rho \cdot V^2$ | MPa (if $\rho$ in kg/m³, $V$ in m/s) | Used for comparative checks |
| 4 | Compressive Strength from $R$ | $f_c = aR + b$ (graphical) | MPa | $a,b$ from manufacturer's chart |
| 5 | Compressive Strength from $V$ | $f_c = k \cdot V^{n}$ (empirical) | MPa | $k,n$ from calibration cores |
| 6 | Rebound Number (corrected) | $R = R_{measured} \pm \Delta_{orientation}$ | dimensionless | Use IS chart |
| 7 | Cover measurement | Read directly from meter (after calibration) | mm | Manufacturer-supplied chart |
| 8 | Standard anvil rebound | $R_{std} = 80 \pm 2$ | dimensionless | For N-type hammer |
| 9 | UPV in steel (theoretical) | $V_{steel} \approx 5900$ m/s | m/s | Why rebar skews UPV |
| 10 | Minimum path length for UPV | $L_{min} = 150$ mm | mm | IS recommendation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The formula $V = L / T$ is the single most-tested equation for this experiment. The examiner will give you $L$ and $T$ and ask for $V$, or give $V$ and $L$ and ask for $T$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pulse Velocity $V = L / T$

The **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity** is defined as the *distance travelled by the pulse per unit time*. The pulse travels a known path length $L$ (in metres) through the concrete and the instrument records the time $T$ (in microseconds) between pulse emission and reception.

**Step 1: Define velocity by kinematics.**

$$V = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the variables relevant to the UPV test.**

$$V = \frac{L}{T}$$

**Step 3: Unit conversion for KTU-standard reporting.**
IS 13311 reports $V$ in **km/s**. Given $L$ in **mm** and $T$ in **$\mu$s**:

$$V \, \text{(km/s)} = \frac{L \, \text{(mm)}}{T \, \text{($\mu$s)}} \times 10^{-3}$$

Equivalently:

$$V \, \text{(m/s)} = \frac{L \, \text{(m)}}{T \, \text{(s)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** $1 \text{ mm} / 1 \text{ $\mu$s} = 10^{-3} \text{ m} / 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1000 \text{ m/s} = 1 \text{ km/s}$. So if $L = 300$ mm and $T = 60$ $\mu$s, then $V = 5$ km/s.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — UPV on a Column

> **Problem:** A concrete column of cross-section $300 \text{ mm} \times 300 \text{ mm}$ is tested by direct UPV. The path length is $L = 300$ mm, and the measured transit time is $T = 68 \, \mu$s. Determine the pulse velocity and the concrete quality as per IS 13311.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert units.**

$$L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.300 \text{ m}, \quad T = 68 \, \mu\text{s} = 68 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Apply $V = L / T$.**

$$V = \frac{0.300 \text{ m}}{68 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}$$

$$V = \frac{0.300}{0.000068} = 4411.76 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Convert to km/s.**

$$V = 4.41 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 4: Classify per IS 13311 Table 2.**

| Range (km/s) | Quality |
|---|---|
| $> 4.5$ | Excellent |
| 3.5 – 4.5 | **Good ← V = 4.41 falls here** |
| 3.0 – 3.5 | Medium |
| < 3.0 | Poor |

**Conclusion:** $V = 4.41$ km/s ⇒ the column concrete is of **GOOD quality**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Rebound Hammer on a Beam

> **Problem:** A rebound hammer test is conducted on a RCC beam (horizontal orientation). Ten readings are taken: 32, 34, 36, 38, 35, 33, 37, 36, 34, 35. The manufacturer's calibration chart gives:
> $f_c = 0.87 R - 17.8$ (MPa). Estimate the compressive strength.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Arrange the readings in ascending order and apply IS 13311 outlier rejection.**

Readings: 32, 33, 34, 34, 35, 35, 36, 36, 37, 38.

- Mean $\bar{R} = \dfrac{32+33+34+34+35+35+36+36+37+38}{10} = \dfrac{350}{10} = 35$.
- Standard deviation: $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})^2}{n-1}}$.
- Deviations: $-3, -2, -1, -1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 3$. Sum of squares = $9+4+1+1+0+0+1+1+4+3 = 24$.
- $\sigma = \sqrt{24/9} = \sqrt{2.667} = 1.633$.

**Step 2: Apply the ±20% rejection criterion (per IS 13311):**
- Lower bound = $\bar{R} - 0.2\bar{R} = 35 - 7 = 28$.
- Upper bound = $\bar{R} + 0.2\bar{R} = 35 + 7 = 42$.

All readings (32 to 38) lie within 28 and 42. **No rejection** required.

**Step 3: Apply the orientation correction.**
Test is **horizontal** ⇒ **no correction needed**.

**Step 4: Compute the corrected rebound number.**

$$R = 35$$

**Step 5: Apply the empirical formula.**

$$f_c = 0.87 \times 35 - 17.8 = 30.45 - 17.8 = 12.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.**
The estimated in-situ compressive strength is approximately **12.65 MPa**, which is very low (poor quality) — further investigation needed.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — Cover Meter Reading

> **Problem:** A cover meter is used on a slab. The instrument is calibrated on a reference block for a 12 mm bar. On scanning the slab, the strongest signal is obtained at point P, with a cover reading of 28 mm. The 3D scan suggests a continuous linear bar. The design drawing specifies a clear cover of 25 mm. Comment on compliance.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compare measured cover with design cover.**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Measured cover | 28 mm |
| Design clear cover | 25 mm |
| Tolerance (IS 456) | $-5$ mm to $+10$ mm typically |
| Effective cover (cover + half bar) | $28 + 6 = 34$ mm |

**Step 2: Comment.**
The measured clear cover of **28 mm** exceeds the specified 25 mm but is within IS 456 tolerance (+10 mm allowable). However, **effective cover (34 mm)** is now larger than the design effective cover (25 + 6 = 31 mm), which means the **lever arm of the reinforcement has increased slightly** — usually a *favourable* deviation from a strength standpoint, but the **crack width may be affected** at service loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment:** Effective cover exceeding design value is generally acceptable, but the engineer must verify flexural capacity and serviceability (crack width) per IS 456:2000.

---

## 3.5 Combined NDT Analysis (CO-5 / Higher-Order Question Pattern)

When both Rebound Hammer and UPV are used on the same element, the KTU model answer requires a **combined interpretation matrix**:

| UPV (km/s) | Rebound $R$ | Likely Condition |
|---|---|---|
| High (>4.0) | High (>40) | Good, dense, strong concrete |
| High (>4.0) | Low (<25) | Carbonated surface / very hard aggregate near surface but weak interior |
| Low (<3.0) | High (>40) | Moisture / surface hardened but interior poor |
| Low (<3.0) | Low (<25) | Poor concrete throughout — investigate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a favourite KTU long-answer (14-mark) question pattern in Module 2 of PCCVL407.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Rebound Hammer Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plunger pressed on concrete surface] --> B[Spring compressed by hammer mass]
    B --> C[Auto-release catch triggered]
    C --> D[Hammer mass strikes plunger]
    D --> E{Energy partition}
    E -->|Absorbed| F[Indentation and plastic deformation]
    E -->|Recovered| G[Rebound of hammer mass]
    G --> H[Slider records max rebound distance]
    H --> I[Rebound Number R displayed]
    I --> J{Anvil check 80 +/- 2 OK?}
    J -->|No| K[Re-calibrate instrument]
    J -->|Yes| L[Apply orientation correction]
    L --> M[Compute mean R of 10 readings]
    M --> N[Use calibration curve f_c vs R]
    N --> O[Estimated compressive strength]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — UPV Test Setup (Direct Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TxUnit[Transmitter Assembly]
        A1[Pulse Generator 54 kHz] --> A2[Transmitting Transducer]
    end
    A2 -->|Ultrasonic pulse| B[Concrete Member]
    B -->|Through concrete| C[Receiving Transducer]
    subgraph RxUnit[Receiver Assembly]
        C --> D1[Amplifier]
        D1 --> D2[Time Counter microsecond]
    end
    D2 --> E[Display V km/s]
    E --> F[Compare with IS 13311 quality table]
    F --> G[Excellent Good Medium Doubtful Poor]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cover Meter (Rebar Locator) Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alternating current in primary coil] --> B[Primary magnetic field generated]
    B --> C{Steel rebar in vicinity?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Eddy currents induced in rebar]
    D --> E[Secondary magnetic field opposes primary]
    E --> F[Impedance of primary coil changes]
    F --> G[Signal processed by instrument]
    G --> H[Cover mm displayed]
    G --> I[Rebar diameter estimated]
    G --> J[Buzzer or LED confirms rebar position]
    C -->|No rebar| K[No signal — no reading]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Three NDT Methods at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[NDT of Hardened Concrete] --> M1[Rebound Hammer]
    Start --> M2[UPV Test]
    Start --> M3[Rebar Locator]
    M1 --> P1[Measures: Surface Hardness]
    M1 --> O1[Output: Rebound Number R]
    M1 --> F1[Estimates: Compressive Strength]
    M2 --> P2[Measures: Pulse Transit Time T]
    M2 --> O2[Output: Velocity V km/s]
    M2 --> F2[Estimates: Density Uniformity Modulus]
    M3 --> P3[Measures: Magnetic Perturbation]
    M3 --> O3[Output: Cover mm Bar Size mm]
    M3 --> F3[Estimates: Rebar Position and Cover]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A labelled diagram of the Rebound Hammer showing the plunger, hammer mass, spring, release catch, and slider is worth **at least 3 marks** in the 14-mark ESE question. Always draw and label.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define Non-Destructive Testing of concrete. List any two NDT methods with the corresponding IS codes.**

**Model Answer:**
Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) refers to the evaluation of *in-situ* properties of hardened concrete **without causing damage** to the structural member. The two principal NDT methods with IS codes are:
1. **Rebound Hammer Test** – **IS 13311 (Part 2) : 1992**
2. **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity Test** – **IS 13311 (Part 1) : 1992**

(Other acceptable: Rebar Locator – IS 13311 Part 3.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**What is the standard anvil rebound value for a Schmidt N-type hammer, and why is it checked before testing?**

**Model Answer:**
The standard anvil rebound value is **80 ± 2**. The anvil check is performed **before** every testing session to verify that the hammer's spring tension and impact energy are within manufacturer-specified tolerances. If the anvil reading deviates, the instrument must be **mechanically re-calibrated**; otherwise, all subsequent field readings will be erroneous.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Describe the **working principle, procedure, and limitations** of the **Rebound Hammer Test** as per IS 13311 (Part 2) : 1992. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A Rebound Hammer test on a horizontal RCC slab gives the readings: 28, 32, 36, 40, 38, 34, 30, 32, 35, 33. The manufacturer's chart gives $f_c = 0.85 R - 16$ (MPa). Estimate the in-situ compressive strength. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks
- **Principle (2 marks):** A spring-loaded hammer mass strikes a plunger in contact with concrete. The rebound distance is read on a scale as the **Rebound Number R**. R is empirically correlated to surface hardness and hence to compressive strength.
- **Procedure (3 marks):**
  1. Calibrate the hammer on the **standard anvil** (R = 80 ± 2).
  2. Smooth the test area; remove loose material.
  3. Hold the hammer **perpendicular** to the surface, in the required orientation.
  4. Take **at least 10 readings** in a grid pattern, **at least 20 mm apart**.
  5. Discard outliers beyond ±20% of the mean.
  6. Apply orientation correction from the IS chart.
  7. Use the manufacturer's chart to estimate $f_c$.
- **Limitations (2 marks):** (i) Tests only the **surface layer (~30 mm)**, not the bulk; (ii) Affected by surface carbonation, moisture, finishing, and orientation; (iii) Gives *estimate*, not exact strength — cores needed for accuracy.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Sort readings:** 28, 30, 32, 32, 33, 34, 35, 36, 38, 40.
**Step 2: Compute mean:**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{28+30+32+32+33+34+35+36+38+40}{10} = \frac{338}{10} = 33.8$$

**Step 3: Compute standard deviation.**
Deviations from mean: $-5.8, -3.8, -1.8, -1.8, -0.8, 0.2, 1.2, 2.2, 4.2, 6.2$.
Squared deviations: $33.64, 14.44, 3.24, 3.24, 0.64, 0.04, 1.44, 4.84, 17.64, 38.44$.
Sum = 117.6.
$\sigma = \sqrt{117.6 / 9} = \sqrt{13.067} = 3.615$.

**Step 4: Apply ±20% rejection rule.**
Lower bound = $33.8 \times 0.8 = 27.04$. Upper bound = $33.8 \times 1.2 = 40.56$.
All readings fall within these bounds. **No rejection.**

**Step 5: Orientation correction.**
Test is **horizontal** ⇒ no correction.

**Step 6: Use the empirical formula.**

$$f_c = 0.85 \times 33.8 - 16 = 28.73 - 16 = 12.73 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7: Conclusion.** The estimated in-situ compressive strength is approximately **12.7 MPa**, indicating *poor-quality concrete*; further investigation (coring, UPV) is recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity test** with a neat diagram. List the velocity criteria as per IS 13311 (Part 1). **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** In a UPV test on a beam, the path length is $L = 500$ mm and transit time $T = 110 \, \mu$s. Comment on the concrete quality. If the density of concrete is 2400 kg/m³, estimate the **dynamic modulus of elasticity** using $E_d = \rho V^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks
- **Principle (2 marks):** Ultrasonic pulses (frequency 20–150 kHz) are generated by a transmitter, pass through concrete, and are received by a receiver. The transit time $T$ is measured, and velocity $V = L/T$ is computed.
- **Diagram (2 marks):** Block diagram of Tx → concrete → Rx → time counter → display.
- **Procedure + Transducer arrangements (2 marks):** Direct, semi-direct, indirect methods. Direct is most reliable.
- **IS 13311 Velocity Criteria (1 mark):**

| V (km/s) | Quality |
|---|---|
| > 4.5 | Excellent |
| 3.5 – 4.5 | Good |
| 3.0 – 3.5 | Medium |
| 2.0 – 3.0 | Doubtful |
| < 2.0 | Poor |

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute pulse velocity.**

$$L = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.500 \text{ m}, \quad T = 110 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

$$V = \frac{0.500}{110 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.500}{0.000110} = 4545.45 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V \approx 4.55 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 2: Classify as per IS 13311.**
$V = 4.55$ km/s > 4.5 km/s ⇒ **Excellent** quality concrete.

**Step 3: Compute dynamic modulus of elasticity.**

$$E_d = \rho V^2$$

With $\rho = 2400$ kg/m³ and $V = 4545.45$ m/s:

$$E_d = 2400 \times (4545.45)^2$$

$$E_d = 2400 \times 20,661,150$$

$$E_d = 4.9586 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} = 49,586 \text{ MPa} \approx 49.6 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion.** The beam is in **excellent** condition with dynamic modulus of about **49.6 GPa** — consistent with high-strength dense concrete.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the 20% outlier rejection rule** for rebound hammer readings → −1 mark.
> 2. **Not converting units** (e.g., mm/μs directly giving m/s instead of km/s) → −1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing V = L/T** with $E_d = \rho V^2$ and writing the wrong formula → −2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the anvil check** step in the rebound hammer procedure → −1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting orientation correction** in the rebound hammer → −1 mark.
> 6. **Mentioning destructive tests** (cube testing) as NDT → −2 marks (KTU is strict on terminology).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NDT** = testing without damage; three principal methods per **IS 13311 (Parts 1–3) : 1992**.
- **Rebound Hammer** → measures **surface hardness** → estimates compressive strength via calibration chart.
- **Standard anvil value = 80 ± 2** for N-type hammer.
- **At least 10 readings**, grid pattern, **20 mm apart**, **±20% rejection rule**, apply **orientation correction**.
- **UPV** → $V = L / T$ → velocity criterion (4.5/3.5/3.0 km/s) for excellent/good/medium/doubtful/poor.
- **Direct transmission** is most reliable; **indirect** is least.
- **Factors affecting UPV**: aggregate, moisture, temperature, rebar, path length, surface.
- **Rebar Locator / Cover Meter** → uses **electromagnetic / eddy current principle** → measures **cover and bar size**.
- **Calibrate on reference block** before cover measurement.
- **Effective cover** = clear cover + half bar diameter.
- **Combined NDT (Rebound + UPV)** gives a more reliable estimate than either alone.
- **NDT gives index, not strength**; coring is the only true measure of in-situ strength (IS 516).
- **IS codes to remember**:
  - IS 13311 (Part 1) : 1992 – UPV
  - IS 13311 (Part 2) : 1992 – Rebound Hammer
  - IS 13311 (Part 3) : 1992 – Rebar Locator
  - IS 516 – Hardened concrete strength (cube/cores)
  - IS 456 : 2000 – Code of practice (cover specification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The most frequently asked long question is *"Describe the Rebound Hammer test with neat sketch and state the factors affecting the rebound number."* Always draw the labelled diagram, list the **calibration procedure**, and tabulate the **orientation correction chart** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study on Concrete Core Cutter, Concrete Penetrometer and Crack Detection Microscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL407 - MATERIAL TESTING LAB II
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study on Concrete Core Cutter, Concrete Penetrometer and Crack Detection Microscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Study on Concrete Core Cutter, Concrete Penetrometer and Crack Detection Microscope

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

This experiment encompasses the study of three essential **non-destructive and partially-destructive field/lab testing instruments** used in the quality assurance and structural health monitoring of hardened concrete in civil engineering practice.

### 1.1 Concrete Core Cutter (Core Drilling Machine)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IS 516 Part 5 / ASTM C42)**
> A *Concrete Core Cutter* is a heavy-duty rotary drilling apparatus fitted with a **diamond-tipped core bit** that extracts cylindrical concrete specimens (cores) of standard diameter (typically **100 mm** or **150 mm**) directly from hardened structural concrete. The extracted core is then tested in a compression testing machine to determine the *in-situ* compressive strength of the parent concrete.

- **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine you baked a large cake and want to check if it's cooked inside without cutting the whole cake. A core cutter is like a "cookie-cutter" that cuts a small cylindrical sample out of the cake, allowing you to test the inside's quality directly. The small hole left behind is later patched with fresh concrete.
- **Key Physical Standards:** Diamond core bits rotate at **300–900 rpm** under water cooling to prevent overheating and dust suppression.

### 1.2 Concrete Penetrometer (Windsor Pin Penetration Test)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (ASTM C803 / IS 8146)**
> A *Concrete Penetrometer* (commonly the *Windsor Probe System*) is a non-destructive testing device that uses a **powder-actuated gun** to drive a hardened **tungsten–cobalt alloy pin** into the concrete surface. The depth of pin penetration is inversely proportional to the compressive strength of the concrete, allowing estimation of in-place strength via calibrated charts.

- **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of trying to push a thumbtack into a piece of soft wood versus a piece of hardwood. The harder the material, the shallower the tack penetrates. The penetrometer works on the same principle: it uses controlled explosive force to shoot a pin, and the depth tells you how "hard" the concrete is.
- **Standard Pin Specs:** Pin length = **79.5 mm**, diameter = **6.4 mm**, made of tungsten carbide alloy.

### 1.3 Crack Detection Microscope (Crack Width Gauge Microscope)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> A *Crack Detection Microscope* is a portable, hand-held optical instrument (typically **30× to 50× magnification**) equipped with a graduated reticle (an internal measuring scale) used to measure the **width of cracks** in concrete, masonry, and stone surfaces with an accuracy of **0.02 mm** (for a 50× model).

- **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** It's like a tiny magnifying glass with a built-in ruler (the reticle). You place it over a crack, look through the eyepiece, and count the number of scale lines covered by the crack. Multiplying this count by the calibration factor gives the exact crack width — essential for engineers deciding whether a crack is merely cosmetic or structurally dangerous.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These three instruments form a complete toolkit — *Core Cutter* gives **quantitative strength** (destructive), *Penetrometer* gives **quick strength estimate** (semi-destructive), and *Crack Microscope* gives **crack geometry** (purely non-destructive).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Concrete Core Cutter — Theory of Operation

The working principle rests on the fact that the compressive strength of a properly extracted and capped core is a direct measure of the in-situ strength of the parent concrete.

**Working Logic Steps:**
1. A diamond-impregnated **annular (hollow cylindrical) cutting bit** is mounted on a rigid drilling rig anchored to the concrete surface.
2. The motor rotates the bit at high speed while simultaneously applying downward thrust; **water is continuously flushed** through the bit for cooling and slurry removal.
3. The annular cutter slices a cylindrical groove, leaving an intact central plug — the **concrete core**.
4. The core is broken off at its base using a wedge or lever, retrieved, and its ends are **capped with sulphur or high-strength capping compound** to ensure a parallel, flat loading surface.
5. The capped core is tested in a **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)** to failure.

**Why Core Length-to-Diameter (L/D) Ratio Matters:**
Standard cylinder strength is measured on cylinders with L/D = 2.0. Cores drilled in the field rarely have exactly L/D = 2.0, so a **correction factor (F)** is applied.

### 2.2 Concrete Penetrometer — Theory of Operation

The penetration depth is correlated to compressive strength using manufacturer-supplied calibration charts (Mohammed's equations or Windsor empirical curves).

**Working Logic Steps:**
1. A *standard power load* (cartridge) is selected based on expected concrete strength.
2. The probe gun is firmly held against the concrete surface, perpendicular to it.
3. The trigger fires the pin into the concrete using a controlled powder charge.
4. After firing, the **exposed length of the pin** is measured using a depth gauge.
5. **Penetration depth = Pin length − Exposed length.**
6. Average penetration is correlated to compressive strength using the **calibration curve**.

**Important Variables Influencing Penetration:**
- Aggregate type and hardness
- Surface moisture
- Concrete age (>14 days preferred for accuracy)
- Rebar presence (must be avoided)

### 2.3 Crack Detection Microscope — Theory of Operation

The instrument is essentially a **portable traveling microscope**. Light is directed at the crack via an internal illuminator; the user looks through an eyepiece that contains a **graduated reticle** (fine scale lines).

**Working Logic Steps:**
1. The microscope is placed with its **measuring tip** (a small 3-legged base) directly on the crack.
2. Internal illumination lights the crack and the reticle scale.
3. The reticle scale is rotated or aligned using a knurled knob so that the scale lines align with the edges of the crack.
4. The user counts the number of reticle divisions covered by the crack width.
5. **Crack Width = (Number of divisions) × (Calibration factor).**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Instrument | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|------------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Core Cutter | $f_{core} = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$ | $P$ = Failure load, $d$ = Core diameter, $A$ = Cross-sectional area | MPa (N/mm²) |
| 2 | Core Cutter | $f_{corrected} = f_{core} \times F$ | $F$ = L/D correction factor | MPa |
| 3 | Core Cutter | $L/D$ ratio check | $L$ = length, $D$ = diameter | dimensionless |
| 4 | Penetrometer | $T = L - P_{exp}$ | $T$ = penetration depth, $L$ = pin length, $P_{exp}$ = exposed length | mm |
| 5 | Penetrometer | $f_{c} = f(T)$ | Empirical function from calibration curve | MPa |
| 6 | Crack Microscope | $w = N \times C$ | $w$ = crack width, $N$ = number of reticle divisions, $C$ = calibration factor | mm |
| 7 | Crack Microscope | $C = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{M}$ | $M$ = total divisions per mm on reticle (e.g., 50 for 0.02 mm) | mm/div |

**L/D Correction Factor Table (IS 516 Part 5):**

| L/D Ratio | Correction Factor (F) |
|-----------|----------------------|
| 2.00 | 1.00 |
| 1.75 | 0.98 |
| 1.50 | 0.96 |
| 1.25 | 0.93 |
| 1.00 | 0.89 |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Core Cutters** are routinely used in **structural audit** of bridges, high-rise buildings, and dams to verify whether the in-situ concrete meets the design strength (M25, M30, etc.).
- **Penetrometers** are invaluable for **quick spot-checks** when cores are not feasible — e.g., on critical columns where drilling may weaken the structure.
- **Crack Microscopes** are mandatory tools in **post-earthquake damage assessment** and in monitoring **progressive crack propagation** in distressed structures.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Concrete Core Cutter Strength Calculation

**Problem Statement (Model Lab Question):**
A concrete core of diameter **100 mm** and length **150 mm** was extracted from a column. The core failed in compression at a load of **240 kN**. Determine the in-situ compressive strength of the concrete. (Assume capping is properly done.)

**Step 1 — Identify the diameter and calculate the cross-sectional area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m} \\
A &= \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \\
A &= \dfrac{\pi \times (100)^{2}}{4} \\
A &= \dfrac{\pi \times 10000}{4} \\
A &= 2500 \pi \text{ mm}^{2} \\
A &\approx 7853.98 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the failure load in Newtons:**

$$
P = 240 \text{ kN} = 240 \times 10^{3} \text{ N} = 240{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the uncorrected core compressive strength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{core} &= \dfrac{P}{A} \\
f_{core} &= \dfrac{240{,}000}{7853.98} \\
f_{core} &\approx 30.56 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 30.56 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the L/D ratio and look up the correction factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L/D &= \dfrac{150}{100} = 1.50
\end{aligned}
$$

From the IS 516 Part 5 table, for L/D = 1.50, **F = 0.96**.

**Step 5 — Apply the correction factor to obtain in-situ strength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{corrected} &= f_{core} \times F \\
f_{corrected} &= 30.56 \times 0.96 \\
f_{corrected} &= 29.34 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The in-situ compressive strength of the concrete is approximately **29.34 MPa**, which is close to M30 grade concrete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** If the design required M25 (25 MPa) concrete, the column passes. If M30 was specified, the result is borderline and warrants a re-test at another location.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Windsor Penetrometer Pin Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
Three pins of length **79.5 mm** each were fired into a concrete slab. The exposed lengths measured were **46.0 mm, 47.5 mm, and 45.0 mm**. Using the calibration chart, the average penetration corresponds to a strength of **28 MPa**. Calculate (a) average exposed length, (b) average penetration depth, and (c) confirm the strength interpretation.

**Step 1 — Compute the average exposed length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{exp, avg} &= \dfrac{46.0 + 47.5 + 45.0}{3} \\
P_{exp, avg} &= \dfrac{138.5}{3} \\
P_{exp, avg} &= 46.17 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average penetration depth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= L - P_{exp, avg} \\
T_{avg} &= 79.5 - 46.17 \\
T_{avg} &= 33.33 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

A penetration depth of 33.33 mm with the standard charge level indicates a compressive strength of approximately **28 MPa** (based on the manufacturer's calibration chart). This is consistent with **M25 grade concrete**.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Crack Detection Microscope Measurement

**Problem Statement:**
A 50× crack detection microscope has a reticle with 50 divisions covering 1 mm. A crack on a beam surface is found to span **7 divisions** of the reticle. Calculate the actual crack width.

**Step 1 — Determine the calibration factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{M} \\
C &= \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ divisions}} \\
C &= 0.02 \text{ mm/division}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the crack width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= N \times C \\
w &= 7 \times 0.02 \\
w &= 0.14 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The crack width is **0.14 mm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Classification per IS 456:2000:** Crack widths up to **0.3 mm** are generally classified as *minor/hairline* cracks for RCC members. However, for water-retaining structures, even 0.14 mm may require sealing.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study

| Feature | Core Cutter | Penetrometer | Crack Microscope |
|---------|-------------|--------------|------------------|
| Test Type | Destructive | Semi-Destructive | Non-Destructive |
| Parameter Measured | Compressive strength | Strength (estimated) | Crack width |
| Output | MPa (direct) | MPa (via chart) | mm |
| Accuracy | High (±5%) | Moderate (±15–20%) | High (±0.02 mm) |
| Cost per Test | High | Moderate | Low |
| Time Required | 1–2 days (capping + testing) | 30–60 minutes | <5 minutes |
| Standards | IS 516 Pt 5, ASTM C42 | ASTM C803, IS 8146 | Manufacturer-specific |
| Typical Use | Audit, acceptance | Quick strength check | Crack monitoring |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Concrete Core Cutter Operational Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Test Location] --> B[Anchor Drilling Rig to Concrete]
    B --> C[Attach Diamond Core Bit 100mm / 150mm]
    C --> D[Engage Water Cooling System]
    D --> E[Drill at 300-900 rpm with Steady Thrust]
    E --> F[Extract Cylindrical Core Specimen]
    F --> G[Break Core Free Using Wedge Tool]
    G --> H[Cap Both Ends with Sulphur Compound]
    H --> I[Measure Core Dimensions L and D]
    I --> J[Compute L/D Ratio]
    J --> K{Is L/D Acceptable?}
    K -- No --> L[Trim or Discard Core]
    K -- Yes --> M[Place in Compression Testing Machine]
    M --> N[Apply Axial Load Until Failure]
    N --> O[Record Peak Load P in kN]
    O --> P[Compute f_core = 4P divided by pi d squared]
    P --> Q[Apply L/D Correction Factor F]
    Q --> R[Final Result: In-situ Compressive Strength in MPa]
    R --> S[End: Report and Patch Core Hole]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Windsor Penetrometer Test Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Visual Inspection of Surface] --> B1[Locate Rebar Using Cover Meter]
    B1 --> C1[Mark 3 Test Points in Triangular Layout]
    C1 --> D1[Select Power Cartridge based on Expected Strength]
    D1 --> E1[Position Probe Gun Perpendicular to Surface]
    E1 --> F1[Fire Pin Using Powder Charge]
    F1 --> G1[Measure Exposed Length of Pin]
    G1 --> H1[Repeat for all 3 Pins]
    H1 --> I1[Compute Average Exposed Length]
    I1 --> J1[Calculate Penetration Depth T = L - P_exp]
    J1 --> K1[Refer to Manufacturer Calibration Chart]
    K1 --> L1[Obtain Estimated Compressive Strength]
    L1 --> M1[End: Document Result with Site Conditions]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Crack Detection Microscope Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OpticalAssembly [Optical System of Microscope]
        L1[Illumination LED] --> M1[Beam Splitter]
        M1 --> S1[Specimen Surface Crack Plane]

        O1 --> R1[Graduated Reticle Internal Scale]

    end

    subgraph MeasurementChain [Measurement Logic]

    U1 --> N1
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (Text-Based Block Diagram)

The complete testing suite can be represented as a hierarchical functional flow:

```
LEVEL 1: INPUT
   ├── Fresh Hardened Concrete Element
   ├── Existing Cracked Surface
   └── Access to Powder-actuated Tools

LEVEL 2: EQUIPMENT ENGAGEMENT
   ├── Diamond Core Drill Machine
   ├── Windsor Pin Probe Gun
   └── Hand-held Crack Microscope

LEVEL 3: DATA ACQUISITION
   ├── Failure Load (kN)  → Core Test
   ├── Exposed Pin Length (mm)  → Penetrometer
   └── Reticle Division Count  → Crack Microscope

LEVEL 4: COMPUTATION
   ├── f_core = 4P / (πd²) × F
   ├── T = L - P_exp → f_c (via chart)
   └── w = N × C

LEVEL 5: OUTPUT
   ├── In-situ Strength Report
   ├── Estimated Strength Map
   └── Crack Width Log (Crack Map)
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three applications of a Concrete Core Cutter in civil engineering quality control.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **In-situ strength evaluation** of hardened concrete in existing structures (columns, beams, slabs) where standard cube/cylinder results are unavailable.
2. **Quality audit of large infrastructure** projects such as bridges, dams, and high-rise buildings to confirm compliance with design-grade specifications.
3. **Investigation of structural distress** — extracting cores from suspect areas to determine actual strength when structures show signs of deterioration or suspected low-quality concrete.
*(3 marks awarded for any three valid points with brief justification.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the principle of the Windsor Pin Penetration Test and the role of the calibration chart.*

**Model Answer:**
**Principle:** A hardened tungsten-carbide alloy pin is driven into hardened concrete by a powder-actuated gun with a known energy level. The **penetration depth is inversely proportional** to the compressive strength of concrete — higher strength = lower penetration, and vice versa.

**Role of Calibration Chart:** The chart is a manufacturer-supplied empirical graph that correlates the measured pin penetration (in mm) to the corresponding compressive strength (in MPa) for specific aggregate types and power charge levels. Without the chart, raw penetration depth cannot be converted to a meaningful strength value.
*(2 marks for principle + 1 mark for chart role.)*

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the construction, working, and procedure of testing concrete using a **Core Cutter**. State the relevant IS/ASTM code.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A concrete core of diameter **150 mm** and length **200 mm** failed at a load of **420 kN** in a compression test. Calculate the **in-situ compressive strength** of the concrete, applying the appropriate correction factor.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Construction, Working & Procedure [7 Marks]:**

**Constructional Features:**
- **Rigid base frame** with provisions for anchoring bolts to fix the machine to the concrete surface.
- **Electric motor or gasoline engine** (typically 1.5–3 HP) coupled to a gear-reduction unit.
- **Vertical spindle** rotating at **300–900 rpm**.
- **Diamond-tipped annular core bit** of standard diameter (100 mm or 150 mm) with water-swivel connector.
- **Water supply system** for continuous cooling and dust suppression.
- **Pressure gauge / thrust control** to regulate downward force on the bit.
- **Carriage and feed mechanism** for vertical movement of the spindle.

**Working Principle:** A hollow cylindrical diamond bit rotates under downward thrust, grinding an annular groove into the concrete. The intact central plug — the **core** — is then broken free at its base, retrieved, capped, and tested in compression.

**Testing Procedure:**
1. Select a **representative location** away from rebar (verified by cover meter) and away from edges/joints.
2. Anchor the drill rig firmly to the surface using expansion bolts.
3. Mount the appropriate diameter diamond bit.
4. Connect water supply; start the motor and gradually engage the bit.
5. Maintain steady, even pressure; allow the bit to advance at **10–15 mm per minute**.
6. Once the full depth of the bit is reached, withdraw the spindle.
7. Break the core free at its base using a **wedge or lever** inserted into the annular slot.
8. Extract the core carefully; **label** it with location and orientation.
9. Cap the ends with **sulphur capping compound** to ensure parallel, flat surfaces.
10. Measure **L** and **D** accurately.
11. Test the core in a CTM with parallel loading platens; record the peak failure load.

**Relevant Codes:** *IS 516 (Part 5):2018* and *ASTM C42 / C42M*.

*(Awarded: 1 mark diagram + 4 marks working + 2 marks procedure; mark split may vary by paper.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- Core diameter, $d = 150$ mm
- Core length, $L = 200$ mm
- Failure load, $P = 420$ kN $= 420{,}000$ N

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \\
A &= \dfrac{\pi \times (150)^{2}}{4} \\
A &= \dfrac{\pi \times 22500}{4} \\
A &= 5625 \pi \\
A &\approx 17671.46 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the uncorrected compressive strength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{core} &= \dfrac{P}{A} \\
f_{core} &= \dfrac{420{,}000}{17671.46} \\
f_{core} &\approx 23.77 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute L/D and select correction factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L/D &= \dfrac{200}{150} = 1.333
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpolating between 1.25 (F = 0.93) and 1.50 (F = 0.96):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 0.93 + \dfrac{(1.333 - 1.25)}{(1.50 - 1.25)} \times (0.96 - 0.93) \\
F &= 0.93 + \dfrac{0.083}{0.25} \times 0.03 \\
F &= 0.93 + 0.333 \times 0.03 \\
F &= 0.93 + 0.01 \\
F &\approx 0.94
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the corrected in-situ strength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{corrected} &= f_{core} \times F \\
f_{corrected} &= 23.77 \times 0.94 \\
f_{corrected} &\approx 22.34 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The in-situ compressive strength of the concrete is **approximately 22.34 MPa**, which is just below the M25 grade specification. The structure would be flagged for further investigation.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for area, 2 marks for f_core, 1 mark for L/D + F, 2 marks for final result.)*

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle, components, and procedure of the **Windsor Pin Penetrometer Test**. Mention the standards followed and limitations of the test.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using a **Crack Detection Microscope** (50× magnification, reticle with 50 divisions per mm), a crack on a column surface was measured. The crack spanned **12 divisions** of the reticle.
(i) Calculate the crack width in mm.
(ii) State the permissible limit of crack width for RCC members as per IS 456:2000 and comment on whether the observed crack is acceptable.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Windsor Pin Penetrometer Test [7 Marks]:**

**Principle:** A powder-actuated probe gun drives a hardened **tungsten-carbide pin** of standard length (79.5 mm) and diameter (6.4 mm) into the concrete surface with a known, calibrated energy from a powder cartridge. The **depth of penetration is inversely related to the in-place compressive strength** of the concrete. The exposed length of the pin is measured after firing, and the corresponding strength is read off a **manufacturer-supplied calibration chart**.

**Components:**
- **Probe gun** (powder-actuated driver).
- **Tungsten-cobalt alloy pins** (3 per test set).
- **Power cartridges / powder loads** (three colour-coded strengths: green for low, yellow for medium, red for high strength concrete).
- **Depth gauge / caliper** to measure exposed pin length.
- **Calibration chart** (Mohammed's or manufacturer's curve).
- **Cover meter** (auxiliary) to detect and avoid rebar.

**Procedure:**
1. Visually inspect the surface; use a **cover meter** to locate reinforcement and avoid it.
2. Mark **three test points** forming a triangle, each ~150–200 mm apart.
3. Select the appropriate power cartridge based on expected concrete strength.
4. Place the probe gun firmly on the first point, perpendicular to the surface.
5. Fire the pin; remove the gun and measure the **exposed length** of the pin with a depth gauge.
6. Repeat for points 2 and 3.
7. Compute the **average exposed length** and then the **average penetration depth**: $T = L - P_{exp}$.
8. Refer to the calibration chart to obtain estimated compressive strength.
9. Report the result along with **site conditions** (concrete age, surface condition, aggregate type).

**Standards:** *ASTM C803 / C803M* and *IS 8146*.

**Limitations:**
- Accuracy is **±15–20%**, lower than core testing.
- The result is **sensitive to aggregate hardness**; local calibration is recommended.
- **Surface hardness** is measured, not bulk strength — meaningful only when properly correlated.
- Pin bending due to encountering rebar or large aggregate leads to discarded results.

*(Marks: 1 principle + 2 components + 3 procedure + 1 standard/limitations.)*

---

**(b) Crack Width Calculation and IS 456 Limit [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- Magnification = 50×
- Reticle: 50 divisions = 1 mm
- Crack spans N = 12 divisions

**(i) Calculation of Crack Width:**

**Step 1 — Calibration factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ divisions}} \\
C &= 0.02 \text{ mm/division}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Crack width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= N \times C \\
w &= 12 \times 0.02 \\
w &= 0.24 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Permissible Limit (IS 456:2000, Clause 35.3):**

| Environment | Max Permissible Crack Width |
|-------------|-----------------------------|
| Mild | 0.3 mm |
| Moderate | 0.2 mm |
| Severe / Very Severe | 0.1 mm |
| Marine (extreme) | 0.1 mm (or treated as severe) |

**Commentary:**
- If the column is in a **mild environment**, 0.24 mm is **acceptable** (just below the 0.3 mm limit).
- If the environment is **moderate or severe**, the crack **exceeds the permissible limit** and requires **sealing by epoxy injection or grouting**, and the column should be monitored for further propagation.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks calibration, 2 marks width calculation, 3 marks IS 456 limit and commentary.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the L/D correction factor** in core strength problems — examiners specifically award 1–2 marks for this step; omitting it caps the maximum at 5/7 even if the rest is correct.
> 2. **Mixing units**: Pin length, exposed length, and penetration must all be in **mm**; load in **N** for MPa result.
> 3. **Crack microscope calibration factor confusion**: Students often use the **magnification** (50) directly instead of computing the **calibration factor** (0.02 mm). Always derive C first.
> 4. **Penetrometer**: Students confuse the *exposed length* with the *penetration depth*. Remember: **Penetration = Pin Length − Exposed Length**.
> 5. **Rebar avoidance**: Forgetting to mention that core locations and penetrometer points should be rebar-free leads to loss of 1 procedure mark.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Concrete Core Cutter** extracts cylindrical concrete cores; uses **diamond-tipped annular bit** under water cooling.
- ✅ The standard formula is $f_{core} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$; **always apply the L/D correction factor (F)** from IS 516 Part 5.
- ✅ Typical core diameters are **100 mm** and **150 mm**; preferred L/D ≈ 2.0; minimum L/D ≥ 1.0.
- ✅ **Windsor Penetrometer** uses a **tungsten-carbide pin (79.5 mm long)** fired by a powder charge; **penetration depth = pin length − exposed length**.
- ✅ Strength is obtained from a **manufacturer calibration chart**; accuracy is **±15–20%**.
- ✅ Rebar must be **avoided** during both core drilling and penetrometer firing (use cover meter).
- ✅ **Crack Detection Microscope** uses a graduated reticle; the **calibration factor C = 1 / M**, where M = divisions per mm.
- ✅ Crack width $w = N \times C$; typical precision is **0.02 mm** at 50× magnification.
- ✅ IS 456:2000 permissible crack widths: **0.3 mm (mild), 0.2 mm (moderate), 0.1 mm (severe)**.
- ✅ Core testing is **destructive**, penetrometer is **semi-destructive**, and crack microscope is **fully non-destructive**.
- ✅ Core Cutter → IS 516 (Part 5) / ASTM C42; Penetrometer → ASTM C803 / IS 8146.
- ✅ Always **cap core ends** with sulphur or high-strength capping compound before CTM testing.
- ✅ The L/D correction factor **decreases** as L/D moves away from 2.0 (i.e., shorter cores give lower reported strength).
- ✅ For Windsor test, **3 pins per location** is the standard practice; report **average** penetration.
- ✅ Remember the three critical **environmental categories** of IS 456:2000 for crack width compliance.
- ✅ In numerical problems, maintain **unit consistency**: N with mm² for MPa; convert kN → N by multiplying by $10^{3}$.
